content: The area of the triangle formed by the co-ordinate axes and a tangent to the curve $x y=\mathrm{a}^2$ at the point $\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ is _______ sq. units (where a, $x_1$ and $y_1$ are non-zero)
explanation:
Since $\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ lies on the curve $x y=\mathrm{a}^2$,

$$ x_1 y_1=\mathrm{a}^2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Now, $x y=\mathrm{a}^2$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-\frac{y}{x} \\ \therefore & \left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{\left(x_1, y_1\right)}=-\frac{y_1}{x_1} \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of the tangent at $\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y-y_1=-\frac{y_1}{x_1}\left(x-x_1\right) \\ & \Rightarrow x y_1+y x_1=2 x_1 y_1 \\ & \Rightarrow x y_1+y x_1=2 \mathrm{a}^2 \,\,\,\,\,\ldots .[\text { From (i)] }\end{aligned} $$

This tangent meets the coordinate axes at

$$ \left(\frac{2 \mathrm{a}^2}{y_1}, 0\right) \text { and }\left(0, \frac{2 \mathrm{a}^2}{x_1}\right) . $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Required area } & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{a}^2}{y_1}\right)\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{a}^2}{x_1}\right) \\ & =\frac{2 \mathrm{a}^4}{x_1 y_1} \\ & =2 \mathrm{a}^2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\text { ....[From (i)] }\end{aligned} $$

content: The minimum value of the slope of the tangent to curve $y=x^3-3 x^2+2 x+93$ is
explanation:
Step 1: Find the slope of the tangent

The slope of the tangent to the curve is found by differentiating $ y=x^3-3x^2+2x+93 $ with respect to $ x $. This means we find $ \frac{dy}{dx} $:

So, $ \frac{dy}{dx} = 3x^2 - 6x + 2 $ Let’s call $\frac{dy}{dx}$ as $ T $.

Step 2: Find where the slope is the smallest

To find the minimum slope, we need to find where $ T $ is smallest. We do this by setting the derivative of $ T $ with respect to $ x $ equal to zero.

First, find $ \frac{dT}{dx} $: $ \frac{dT}{dx} = 6x - 6 $

Step 3: Solve for $ x $ where the slope is minimum

Set $\frac{dT}{dx} = 0$:

$ 6x - 6 = 0 \implies x = 1 $

Step 4: Make sure it is a minimum

Now, check the second derivative to make sure this is a minimum point: $ \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 6 $ Since $ 6 > 0 $, the function has a minimum at $ x = 1 $.

Step 5: Find the minimum value of the slope

Plug $ x = 1 $ back into the slope formula: $ T = 3(1)^2 - 6(1) + 2 = 3 - 6 + 2 = -1 $

So, the minimum value of the slope of the tangent is $-1$.

content: A spherical balloon is filled with $4500 \pi$ cubic meters of helium gas. If a leak in the balloon causes the gas to escape at the rate of $72 \pi$ cubic meters per minute, then the rate (in meters per minute) at which the radius of the balloon decreases 49 minutes after the leakage has begun, is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d V}{d t}=4 \pi r^2 \frac{d r}{d t} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

After $49 \mathrm{~min},(4500-49 \times 72) \pi=972 \pi \mathrm{~m}^3$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 972 \pi=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \\ \therefore & r^3=3 \times 243=3 \times 3^5 \\ \therefore & r=9 \end{array} $$

Given, $\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dt}}=72 \pi$

$\therefore \quad 72 \pi=4 \pi \times 9 \times 9\left(\frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)\,\,\,….[From (i)]$

$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{2}{9}$

content: If $x$ and $y$ are sides of two squares such that $y=x-x^2$, then the rate of change of area of the second square with respect to that of the first square is
explanation:
Area of the first square $\left(A_1\right)=x^2$

Area of the second square $\left(\mathrm{A}_2\right)=y^2$

$\mathrm{A}_1=x^2$ and $\mathrm{A}_2=y^2$

$$ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dA}_1}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 x \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \text { and } \frac{\mathrm{dA}_2}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{dt}} $$

$$ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dA}_2}{\mathrm{dA}_1}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{dA}_2}{\mathrm{dt}}}{\frac{\mathrm{dA} \mathrm{~A}_1}{\mathrm{dt}}}=\frac{2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}}{2 x \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}} $$

$$ =\frac{y}{x}\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right) $$

Given $y=x-x^2$

$$ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=1-2 x $$

$$ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dA}_2}{\mathrm{dA}_1}=\frac{y}{x}(1-2 x) $$

$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =\frac{x-x^2}{x}(1-2 x) $$

$$ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=(1-x)(1-2 x)=2 x^2-3 x+1 $$

content: The function $\mathrm{f}(x)=[x(x-2)]^2$ is increasing in the set
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{f}(x) & =[x(x-2)]^2 \\ & =x^2(x-2)^2 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) & =2 x(x-2)^2+2(x-2) x^2 \\ & =2 x(x-2)(x-2+x) \\ & =4 x(x-1)(x-2) \end{aligned} $$

For $\mathrm{f}(x)$ to be increasing, $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)>0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 4 x(x-1)(x-2)>0 \\ & \Rightarrow x(x-1)(x-2)>0 \\ & \Rightarrow x \in(0,1) \cup(2, \infty) \end{aligned} $$

content: The minimum value of $a x+b y$ where $x y=c^2$ is
explanation:
$$ \text { } \begin{aligned}Let\,\,\,\, \mathrm{p} & =\mathrm{a} x+\mathrm{b} y \\ & =\mathrm{a} x+\mathrm{b}\left(\frac{\mathrm{c}^2}{x}\right) \quad \ldots\left[\because x y=\mathrm{c}^2\right] \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \mathrm{p}=\mathrm{a} x+\frac{\mathrm{bc}^2}{x} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{dp}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{a}-\frac{\mathrm{bc}^2}{x^2} \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 \mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{bc}^2}{x^3} \end{array} $$

Now, $\frac{\mathrm{dp}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}-\frac{\mathrm{bc}^2}{x^2}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2=\frac{\mathrm{bc}^2}{\mathrm{a}} \\ & \Rightarrow x= \pm \mathrm{c} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~b}}{\mathrm{a}}} \\ & \text { At } x=\mathrm{c} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~b}}{\mathrm{a}}}, \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 \mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}>0 \\ & \text { At } x=-\mathrm{c} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~b}}{\mathrm{a}}}, \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 \mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}<0 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{p}$ is minimum at $x=\mathrm{c} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}}$.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Minimum value } & =a c \sqrt{\frac{b}{a}}+\frac{b c^2}{c \sqrt{\frac{b}{a}}} \quad \ldots[\text { From (i) }] \\ & =c \sqrt{a b}+c \sqrt{a b} \\ & =2 c \sqrt{a b} \end{aligned} $$

content: The equation of the tangent to the curve $y=\mathrm{be}^{-x / \mathrm{a}}$ at the point where it crosses the Y axis is
explanation:
Since the given curve crosses the Y -axis, we put $x=0$ in $y=\mathrm{be}^{-x / \mathrm{a}}$. Then, we get

$$ y=\mathrm{b} $$

$\therefore \quad$ The given curve crosses the Y -axis at $(0, \mathrm{~b})$. Now, $y=\mathrm{be}^{-x / \mathrm{a}}$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{b}{a} e^{-\frac{x}{a}} \\ \therefore & \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(0, b)}=-\frac{b}{a} \end{array} $$

$\therefore \quad$ The equation of the tangent at $(0, b)$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y-\mathrm{b}=-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}(x-0) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{y}{\mathrm{~b}}=1 \end{aligned} $$

content: A manufacturer sells $x$ items at a price of rupees $\left(6-\frac{x}{40}\right)$ each. The cost price of $x$ items is ₹ $\left(\frac{x}{5}+193\right)$. The maximum profit in ₹ __________ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \text { C.P. of } x \text { items }=Rs \left(\frac{x}{5}+193\right) \\ & \text { S.P. of } 1 \text { item }=Rs \left(6-\frac{x}{40}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & \text { S.P. of } x \text { items }=Rs \left(6 x-\frac{x^2}{40}\right) \\ & \text { Profit }=\text { S.P. }- \text { C.P. } \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{P} & =6 x-\frac{x^2}{40}-\left(\frac{x}{5}+193\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}=\frac{-x^2}{40}+\frac{29 x}{5}-193 \end{aligned} $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{-x}{20}+\frac{29}{5} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

For maximum profit, $\frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{-x}{20}+\frac{29}{5}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x}{20}=\frac{29}{5} \\ & \Rightarrow x=116 \end{aligned} $$

Differentiating (i) w.r.t. $x$, we get $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{d} x^2}=\frac{-1}{20}<0$

$\therefore \quad$ Profit is maximum when $x=116$

$$ \begin{aligned} & P=\frac{-x^2}{40}+\frac{29 x}{5}-193=\frac{-116^2}{40}+\frac{29 \times 116}{5}-193 \\ & =116 \times 29\left(\frac{1}{5}-\frac{1}{10}\right)-193 \\ & =336.4-193=Rs 143.4 \end{aligned} $$

content: The equation of the tangent to the curve $\left(1+x^2\right) y=2-x$, where it crosses the X -axis, is
explanation:
Given equation of curve is

$$ \left(1+x^2\right) y=2-x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Since it crosses X -axis $\Rightarrow y=0$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \left(1+x^2\right) 0=2-x \\ & \Rightarrow x=2 \end{array} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Point of contact $\equiv(2,0)$

Differentiating (i) w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(1+x^2\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+y(2 x)=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-(1+2 x y)}{1+x^2} \\ & \left.\frac{d y}{d x}\right|_{(2,0)}=\frac{-1}{5} \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore$ Equation of tangent at $(2,0)$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y-0=\frac{-1}{5}(x-2) \\ & \Rightarrow 5 y=-x+2 \\ & \Rightarrow x+5 y=2 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $y=\alpha \log x+\beta x^3-x$ has extreme values at $x=-1$ and $x=1$, then $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are respectively
explanation:
$$ y=\alpha \log x+\beta x^3-x $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\alpha}{x}+3 \beta x^2-1 $$

For extreme values,

$$ \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \text { at } x=-1 \text { and } x=1 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{\alpha}{-1}+3 \beta-1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-\alpha+3 \beta=1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \text { and } \alpha+3 \beta-1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha+3 \beta=1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{array}\\ &\text { On solving (i) and (ii), we get }\\ &\beta=\frac{1}{3}, \alpha=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: The length of the perpendicular drawn from the origin on the normal to the curve $x^2+2 x y-3 y^2=0$ at the point $(2,2)$ is
explanation:
$$ x^2+2 x y-3 y^2=0 $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 x+2 y+2 x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-3\left(2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-3 y) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-x-y \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-x-y}{x-3 y} \end{aligned} $$

Slope of the normal at $(2,2)$ is

$$ \frac{-1}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(2,2)}}=-1 $$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of the normal at $(2,2)$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y-2=-1(x-2) \\ & \Rightarrow x+y=4 \end{aligned} $$

Length of perpendicular

$$ =\left|\frac{0+0-4}{\sqrt{2}}\right|=\frac{4}{\sqrt{2}}=2 \sqrt{2} \text { units } $$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=\log (1+x)-\frac{2 x}{2+x}$ then $\mathrm{f}(x)$ is increasing in
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=\log (1+x)-\frac{2 x}{2+x} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+x}-\frac{(2+x)(2)-2 x(1)}{(2+x)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{x^2}{(x+1)(x+2)^2} \end{aligned}\\ &\mathrm{f}(x) \text { is increasing, if }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)>0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{(x+1)(x+2)^2}>0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \text { Since } x^2>0,(x+2)^2>0 \\ \therefore \quad & (x+1)>0 \\ & \text { i.e. } x>-1 \end{array} $$

content: The angle $\theta$, at which the curves $y=3^x$ and $y=7^x$ intersect, is given by
explanation:
The point of intersection of the given curves is $(0,1)$.

Now, $y=3^x$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{d y}{d x}=3^x \log 3 \\ \therefore & \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(0,1)}=\log 3=m_1 \text { (say) } \\ & \text { Also, } y=7^x \\ \therefore & \frac{d y}{d x}=7^x \log 7 \\ \therefore & \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(0,1)}=\log 7=m_2 \text { (say) } \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \tan \theta & =\left|\frac{m_1-m_2}{1+m_1 m_2}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{\log 3-\log 7}{1+(\log 3)(\log 7)}\right| \\ & =\frac{\log 3-\log 7}{1+(\log 3)(\log 7)} \text { or } \frac{\log 7-\log 3}{1+(\log 3)(\log 7)} \\ & =\frac{\log \left(\frac{3}{7}\right)}{1+(\log 3)(\log 7)} \text { or } \frac{\log \left(\frac{7}{3}\right)}{1+(\log 3)(\log 7)} \end{aligned} $$

content: The function $\mathrm{f}(x)=x^3-6 x^2+\mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{b}$ satisfies the conditions of Rolle's theorem in $[1,3]$. Then the values of $a$ and $b$ are respectively
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} \,\,\,\,\,\ \quad f(x) =x^3-6 x^2+a x+b \\ \therefore \quad f(1) =1^3-6(1)^2+a(1)+b=a+b-5 \\ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,f(3) =3^3-6(3)^2+a(3)+b=3 a+b-27 \end{array} $$

Since $\mathrm{f}(x)$ satisfies the conditions of Rolle's theorem.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & f(1)=f(3) \\ & \Rightarrow a+b-5=3 a+b-27 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 a=22 \\ & \Rightarrow a=11 \end{array} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Only option (A) satisfies Rolle's theorem.

content: $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x}, x \neq 0$ is strictly decreasing in
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x}, x \neq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^2} \end{aligned} $$

$\mathrm{f}(x)$ is strictly decreasing, if $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)<0$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^2}<0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2<4 \text { and } x \neq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow x \in(-2,0) \cup(0,2) \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Option (D) is the correct answer. } \end{aligned} $$

content: The rate of change of the volume of a sphere with respect to its surface area, when the radius is 5 m is
explanation:
Volume of a sphere $(V)=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3$

Surface area of sphere $(A)=4 \pi r^2$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{d V}{d r}=4 \pi r^2 \text { and } \frac{d A}{d r}=8 \pi r \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d V}{d A}=\frac{\frac{d V}{d r}}{\frac{d A}{d r}}=\frac{4 \pi r^2}{8 \pi r}=\frac{r}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad\left(\frac{d V}{d A}\right)_{r=5}=\frac{5}{2} m \end{aligned} $$

content: If the line $a x+b y+c=0$ is normal to the curve $x y=1$, then
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & x y=1 \\ \therefore & y=\frac{1}{x} \\ \therefore & y^{\prime}=\frac{-1}{x^2} \end{array} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Slope of the normal $=x^2$

Slope of the line $\mathrm{a} x+\mathrm{b} y+\mathrm{c}=0$ is $\frac{-\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}$.

Since the line $\mathrm{a} x+\mathrm{b} y+\mathrm{c}=0$ is a normal to the curve $x y=1$,

$$ x^2=-\frac{a}{b} $$

For this condition to hold true, either $\mathrm{a}<0, \mathrm{~b}>0$ or $b<0, a>0$

content: The sum of two nonzero numbers is 4 . The minimum value of the sum of their reciprocals is
explanation:
Let $x+y=4 \Rightarrow y=4-x$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{y}=\frac{x+y}{x y} \\ & \mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{4}{x y}=\frac{4}{x(4-x)}=\frac{4}{4 x-x^2} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{-4}{\left(4 x-x^2\right)^2} \cdot(4-2 x) \end{aligned} $$

For maximum or minimum of $\mathrm{f}(x)$,

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow 4-2 x=0 \\ \therefore & x=2 \text { and } y=2 \\ \therefore \quad & \min \left(\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{y}\right)=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=1 \end{array} $$

content: The combined equation of the tangent and normal to the curve $x y=15$ at the point $(5,3)$ is________
explanation:
Given equation of curve is

$$ x y=15 $$

Differentiating w.r.to $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-y}{x} . \end{aligned} $$

At $(5,3) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-3}{5}$

Slope of tangent $=\frac{-3}{5}$

Slope of normal $=\frac{5}{3}$

Equation of tangent at $(5,3)$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y-3=\frac{-3}{5}(x-5) \\ & \Rightarrow 3 x+5 y-30=0 \end{aligned} $$

Equation of normal at $(5,3)$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y-3=\frac{5}{3}(x-5) \\ & \Rightarrow 5 x-3 y-16=0 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Combined equation is

$$ \begin{aligned} & (3 x+5 y-30)(5 x-3 y-16)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 15 x^2+16 x y-15 y^2-198 x+10 y+480=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: The length and breadth of a rectangle are $x_{x \mathrm{~cm}}$ and $y \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively. If the length decreases at the rate of $5 \mathrm{~cm} /$ minute and the breadth increases at the rate of $3 \mathrm{~cm} /$ minute, then the rates of change of the perimeter and area respectively when the length is 5 cm and breadth is 2 cm , are
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Perimeter of rectangle }(\mathrm{P})=2(x+y)\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2\left(\frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dt}}+\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}\right) \\ \therefore & \left.\frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right|_{(5,2)}=2(-5+3) \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=-4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{min}\\ &\text { Area of rectangle }(A)=x y\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}} & =x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}+y \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ \left.\frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right|_{(5,2)} & =5(3)+2(-5) \\ & =5 \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{min} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The maximum value of $x^{2 / 3}+(x-2)^{2 / 3}$ is
explanation:
Step 1: Let’s set up the problem.

Let $$y = x^{2/3} + (x-2)^{2/3}$$. We want to find the maximum value of $ y $.

Step 2: Find the first derivative.

We calculate the derivative of $ y $ with respect to $ x $:

$$ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{2}{3} x^{-1/3} + \frac{2}{3} (x-2)^{-1/3} $$

Step 3: Set the derivative to zero to find maximum/minimum points.

Set $ \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 $:

$$ \frac{2}{3} \left( x^{-1/3} + (x-2)^{-1/3} \right) = 0 $$

That means:

$$ x^{-1/3} + (x-2)^{-1/3} = 0 $$

This can also be written as:

$$ \frac{1}{x^{1/3}} + \frac{1}{(x-2)^{1/3}} = 0 $$

So,

$$ (x-2)^{1/3} = -x^{1/3} $$

If we cube both sides, we get:

$$ x - 2 = -x $$

Combine like terms:

$$ x + x = 2 $$

$$ 2x = 2 $$

$$ x = 1 $$

Step 4: Check if this point is a maximum.

We find the second derivative:

$$ \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -\frac{2}{9} x^{-4/3} - \frac{2}{9} (x-2)^{-4/3} $$

When $ x = 1 $, notice that both terms are negative, so the second derivative is less than 0:

$$ \left( \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} \right)_{x=1} < 0 $$

This means $ y $ is at a maximum when $ x = 1 $.

Step 5: Find the maximum value.

Substitute $ x = 1 $ into the original expression for $ y $:

$$ y = 1^{2/3} + (1-2)^{2/3} $$

$1^{2/3} = 1$ and $(-1)^{2/3} = 1$ (since raising -1 to an even power gives 1)

So,

$$ y = 1 + 1 = 2 $$

Final Answer: The maximum value is 2.

content: The point on the curve $4 y^2-4 y+2 x-1=0$ at which the tangent becomes parallel to Y -axis is
explanation:
$$ 4 y^2-4 y+2 x-1=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $y$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 8 y-4+\frac{2 d x}{d y}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=2-4 y \end{aligned} $$

Since the tangent is parallel to Y -axis, $\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 2-4 y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned} $$

From (i),

$$ \begin{aligned} & 4\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2-4\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+2 x-1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=1 \end{aligned} $$

content: A particle moves along a curve $y=\frac{2 x^3-1}{3}$. The points on the curve at which the $y$ co-ordinate is changing 18 times the $x$ co-ordinate are
explanation:
According to the given condition,

$$ \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}=18 \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Given, $y=\frac{2 x^3-1}{3}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{2}{3}\left(3 x^2 \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 x^2 \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \Rightarrow 18 \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 x^2 \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}\,\,\,\,\, …[From (i)]\\ & \Rightarrow x^2=9 \\ & \Rightarrow x= \pm 3 \end{aligned} $$

Putting $x=3$ in (ii), we get

$$ y=\frac{2(3)^3-1}{3}=\frac{53}{3} $$

Putting $x=-3$ in (ii), we get

$$ y=\frac{2(-3)^3-1}{3}=\frac{-55}{3} $$

content: The equation of motion of the particle is $\mathrm{s}=\mathrm{at}^2+\mathrm{bt}+\mathrm{c}$. If the displacement after 1 second is 20 m , velocity after 2 seconds is $30 \mathrm{~m} /$ seconds and the acceleration is $10 \mathrm{~m} /$ seconds $^2$, then
explanation:
$$ s=a t^2+b t+c $$

Displacement after 1 second is 20 m .

$$ \therefore \quad 20=a+b+c \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

$$ \text { Velocity }=v=\frac{d s}{d t}=2 a t+b $$

Velocity after 2 seconds is $30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$

$$ \therefore \quad 30=4 a+b \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)$$

$$ \text { Acceleration }=\frac{d v}{d t}=2 a $$

Acceleration is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}^2$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 10=2 a \\ & \Rightarrow a=5 \end{aligned} $$

From (ii), we get $b=10$

From (i), we get $\mathrm{c}=5$

$$ \therefore \quad a+c=b $$

content: Let $f$ be a function which is continuous and differentiable for all $x$. If $\mathrm{f}(1)=1$ and $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) \leq 5$ for all $x$ in $[1,5]$, then the maximum value of $\mathrm{f}(5)$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { By Lagrange's mean value theorem, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a} \\ & \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{f(5)-f(1)}{5-1} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{f(5)-1}{4} \leq 5 \\ & \Rightarrow f(5)-1 \leq 20 \\ & \Rightarrow f(5) \leq 21 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The function $\mathrm{f}(x)=\sin ^4 x+\cos ^4 x$ increases if
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=\sin ^4 x+\cos ^4 x \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=4 \sin ^3 x \cos x-4 \cos ^3 x \sin x \\ & =-4 \sin x \cos x\left(\cos ^2 x-\sin ^2 x\right) \\ & =-2 \sin 2 x \cos 2 x \\ & =-\sin 4 x \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Function is increasing when } \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) > 0\\ &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow-\sin 4 x > 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin 4 x < 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \pi < 4 x < 2 \pi \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}< x <\frac{\pi}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The normal to the curve $x=9(1+\cos \theta)$, $y=9 \sin \theta$ at $\theta$ always passes through the fixed point
explanation:
$$ x=9(1+\cos \theta), \quad y=9 \sin \theta $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $\theta$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{d x}{d \theta}=-9 \sin \theta, \quad \frac{d y}{d \theta}=9 \cos \theta \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=-\cot \theta \\ & \text { Slope of normal }=\frac{-1}{\frac{d y}{d x}}=\frac{-1}{-\cot \theta}=\tan \theta \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of normal is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{y-9 \sin \theta}{x-9(1+\cos \theta)}=\tan \theta \\ & \Rightarrow y-9 \sin \theta=\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}[x-9(1+\cos \theta)] \\ & \Rightarrow x \sin \theta-y \cos \theta=9 \sin \theta \end{aligned} $$

Only option (A) satisfies the equation of normal.

content: An open tank with a square bottom is to contain 4000 cubic cm . of liquid. The dimensions of the tank so that the surface area of the tank is minimum, is
explanation:
Let the length of the side of square bottom be $x \mathrm{~cm}$ and the height be h cm

Volume $(\mathrm{V})=x^2 \mathrm{~h}=4000$

$$ \Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=\frac{4000}{x^2} $$

Surface area $(A)=x^2+4 x \mathrm{~h}$

$$ \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}=x^2+\frac{16000}{x} $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 x-\frac{16000}{x^2} $$

A is minimum if $\frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 2 x-\frac{16000}{x^2}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^3=8000 \\ & \Rightarrow x=20 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=2+\frac{32000}{x^3} \\ & \left.\frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}\right|_{x=20}=6>0 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Area is minimun when $x=20 \mathrm{~cm}$

$$ \mathrm{h}=\frac{4000}{x^2}=\frac{4000}{400}=10 \mathrm{~cm} $$

content: If $x$ is real, then the difference between the greatest and least values of $\frac{x^2-x+1}{x^2+x+1}$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } y=\frac{x^2-x+1}{x^2+x+1} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\left(x^2+x+1\right)(2 x-1)-\left(x^2-x+1\right)(2 x+1)}{\left(x^2+x+1\right)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{2 x^2-2}{\left(x^2+x+1\right)^2} \end{aligned} $$

For maximum or minimum,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x^2-2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=-1,1 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{4\left(-x^3+3 x+1\right)}{x^2+x+1} \end{aligned} $$

At $x=-1, \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}<0$, the function will occupy maximum value.

$$ \therefore \quad f(-1)=3 $$

At $x=1, \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}>0$, the function will occupy minimum value.

$$ \therefore \quad f(1)=\frac{1}{3} $$

Max. value - Min. value $=3-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{8}{3}$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=x \cdot \mathrm{e}^{x(1-x)}$, then $\mathrm{f}(x)$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{f}(x) & =x \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}(1-x)} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) & =x \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}(1-x)}[x(-1)+(1-x)]+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}(1-x)} \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}(1-x)}\left(x-2 x^2+1\right) \end{aligned} $$

For $\mathrm{f}(x)$ to be increasing, $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) \geq 0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{x(1-x)}\left(x-2 x^2+1\right) \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow x-2 x^2+1 \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x^2-x-1 \leq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow(2 x+1)(x-1) \leq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow x \in\left[-\frac{1}{2}, 1\right] \end{aligned} $$

For $\mathrm{f}(x)$ to be decreasing, $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) \leq 0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(2 x+1)(x-1) \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow x \in\left(-\infty,-\frac{1}{2}\right] \cup[1, \infty) \end{aligned} $$

content: The approximate value of $\sqrt[3]{64 \cdot 04}$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} \text { Let, } \mathrm{f}(x) & =\sqrt[3]{x} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) & =\frac{1}{3} x^{\frac{-2}{3}}=\frac{1}{3 x^{2 / 3}} \\ \quad \text { Here, } \mathrm{a} & =64 \text { and } \mathrm{h}=0.04 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{h}) & \approx \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})+\mathrm{h} \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{a}) \\ & \approx \sqrt[3]{64}+0.04\left[\frac{1}{3(64)^{2 / 3}}\right] \\ & \approx 4+(0.04) \times \frac{1}{48} \\ & \approx 4+(0.04) \times(0.02083) \\ & \approx 4+0.00083 \\ & \approx 4.00083 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{\mathrm{k} \sin x+2 \cos x}{\sin x+\cos x}$ is strictly increasing for all real values of $x$, then
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &f(x)=\frac{k \sin x+2 \cos x}{\sin x+\cos x}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get } \end{aligned} $$

$$ \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{(\sin x+\cos x) \times(k \cos x-2 \sin x)-(\mathrm{k} \sin x+2 \cos x) \times(\cos x-\sin x)}{(\sin x+\cos x)^2} $$

$$ =\frac{\left(\mathrm{k} \sin x \cos x-2 \sin ^2 x+\mathrm{k} \cos ^2 x-2 \sin x \cos x\right)-\left(\mathrm{k} \sin x \cos x-\mathrm{k} \sin ^2 x+2 \cos ^2 x-2 \sin x \cos x\right)}{(\sin x+\cos x)^2} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{(\mathrm{k}-2) \sin ^2 x+(\mathrm{k}-2) \cos ^2 x}{(\sin x+\cos x)^2} \\ & =\frac{(\mathrm{k}-2)\left(\sin ^2 x+\cos ^2 x\right)}{(\sin x+\cos x)^2} \\ & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\mathrm{k}-2}{(\sin x+\cos x)^2} \end{aligned} $$

function $\mathrm{f}(x)$ is strictly increasing       …[Given]

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)>0 \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{k}-2}{(\sin x-\cos x)^2}>0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}-2>0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}>2 \end{array} $$

content: The abscissae of the points of the curve $y=x^3$ are in the interval $[-2,2]$, where the slope of the tangents can be obtained by mean value theorem for the interval $[-2,2]$ are
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & y=x^3=f(x) \\ \therefore \quad & f(2)=8, f(-2)=-8 \text { and } f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2 \end{array}\\ &\text { By mean value theorem, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\mathrm{f}(2)-\mathrm{f}(-2)}{2-(-2)} \\ & \Rightarrow 3 x^2=\frac{8-(-8)}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow x^2=\frac{4}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow x= \pm \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: Let $x$ be the length of each of the equal sides of an isosceles triangle and $\theta$ be the angle between these sides. If $x$ is increasing at the rate $\frac{1}{12} \mathrm{~m} /$ hour and $\theta$ is increasing at the rate $\frac{\pi}{180} \mathrm{rad} /$ hour, then the rate at which area of the triangle is increasing when $x=12 \mathrm{~m}$ and $\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}$ is
explanation:
Given, $\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{12} \mathrm{~m} /$ hour

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\pi}{180} \mathrm{rad} / \text { hour } \\ & x=12 \mathrm{~m}, \theta=\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned} $$

Area of isosceles triangle $(A)=\frac{1}{2} x^2 \sin \theta$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Differentiating w.r.t } x \text { ' } t \text { ', we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}} & =\frac{1}{2}\left[x^2 \cos \theta \frac{\mathrm{~d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}+\sin \theta(2 x) \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[(12)^2 \cos \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{\pi}{180}\right)+\sin \frac{\pi}{4}(2 \times 12)\left(\frac{1}{12}\right)\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{144 \pi}{180} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times 2\right] \\ & =\frac{144 \pi}{360} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & =\sqrt{2}\left[\frac{144 \pi}{360} \times \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}\right] \\ & =\sqrt{2}\left[\frac{\pi}{5}+\frac{1}{2}\right] \mathrm{m}^2 / \text { hour } \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: A wire of length 8 units is cut into two parts which are bent respectively in the form of a square and a circle. The least value of the sum of the areas so formed is
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & 4 x+2 \pi r=8 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & A=x^2+\pi r^2=\frac{1}{16}(8-2 \pi r)^2+\pi r^2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots[\text { From (i) }]\\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d A}{d r}=-\frac{1}{4}(8-2 \pi r) \pi+2 \pi r \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d A}{d r}=0 \text { gives } r=\frac{8}{2(\pi+4)}, \text { thus } \frac{d^2 A}{d r^2}>0 \end{array} $$

and hence minimum.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 4 x=8-2 \pi r=8-\frac{8}{\pi+4}=\frac{32}{\pi+4} \\ \therefore & x=\frac{8}{\pi+4} \\ \therefore & A=x^2+\pi r^2=\frac{64}{4(\pi+4)}=\frac{16}{\pi+4} \end{array} $$

content: The radius of the base of a cone is increasing at the rate $3 \mathrm{~cm} /$ minute and the altitude is decreasing at the rate $4 \mathrm{~cm} /$ minute . The rate at which the lateral surface area is changing, when the radius is 7 cm and altitude is 24 cm is
explanation:
Given, $\frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}=3 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{min}, \frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{min}$

$$ \mathrm{r}=7 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{~h}=24 \mathrm{~cm} $$

Lateral surface area of cone $=A=\pi r \sqrt{r^2+h^2}$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{d A}{d t}=\pi\left[\mathrm{r} \frac{d}{d t} \sqrt{\mathrm{r}^2+\mathrm{h}^2}+\sqrt{\mathrm{r}^2+\mathrm{h}^2} \times \frac{d r}{d t}\right] \\ & =\pi\left[\mathrm{r} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\mathrm{r}^2+\mathrm{h}^2}} \times\left(2 \mathrm{r} \frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}+2 \mathrm{~h} \frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)+\sqrt{\mathrm{r}^2+\mathrm{h}^2} \frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right] \\ & =\pi\left[7 \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{7^2+24^2}} \times(2 \times 7 \times 3+2 \times 24 \times-4)\right. \left.+\sqrt{7^2+24^2} \times 3\right] \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\pi\left[7 \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{625}}(42-192)+\sqrt{625} \times 3\right] \\ & =\pi\left[7 \times \frac{1}{2 \times 25} \times(-150)+25 \times 3\right] \\ & =\pi[-21+75] \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}}=54 \pi \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{min} . \end{aligned} $$

content: The function $x^5-5 x^4+5 x^3-10$ has a maximum, when $x$ is equal to
explanation:
Let the given function be

$$f(x) = x^5 - 5x^4 + 5x^3 - 10.$$

To find the maximum, we need to:

  1. Find the first derivative, $f'(x)$,

  2. Set $f'(x) = 0$ and solve for $x$ (critical points),

  3. Use the second derivative test.

Step 1: Differentiate $f(x)$

$ f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(x^5 - 5x^4 + 5x^3 - 10) $

$ = 5x^4 - 20x^3 + 15x^2 $

Step 2: Equate $f'(x)$ to zero

$ 5x^4 - 20x^3 + 15x^2 = 0 $

Divide both sides by $5$:

$ x^4 - 4x^3 + 3x^2 = 0 $

Factor $x^2$:

$ x^2(x^2 - 4x + 3) = 0 $

Factor the quadratic:

$ x^2(x - 1)(x - 3) = 0 $

So, critical points are $x = 0, 1, 3$.

Step 3: Use second derivative test

First, calculate the second derivative:

$ f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(5x^4 - 20x^3 + 15x^2) $

$ = 20x^3 - 60x^2 + 30x $

Now, substitute the critical points:

  • For $x=0$:

    $ f''(0) = 20(0)^3 - 60(0)^2 + 30(0) = 0 $

    Inconclusive.

  • For $x=1$:

    $ f''(1) = 20(1)^3 - 60(1)^2 + 30(1) $

    $ = 20 - 60 + 30 = -10 $

    Since $f''(1) < 0$, maximum at $x = 1$.

  • For $x=3$:

    $ f''(3) = 20(27) - 60(9) + 30(3) $

    $ = 540 - 540 + 90 = 90 $

    Since $f''(3) > 0$, minimum at $x = 3$.

Final answer:

$ \boxed{1} $

So, the function has a maximum at $x = 1$, i.e., Option B.

content: The function f defined by $\mathrm{f}(x)=(x+2) \mathrm{e}^{-x}$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{f}(x)=(x+2) \mathrm{e}^{-x} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\mathrm{e}^{-x}-\mathrm{e}^{-x}(x+2)=-\mathrm{e}^{-x}(x+1) \end{array} $$

For $\mathrm{f}(x)$ to be increasing,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \quad-\mathrm{e}^{-x}(x+1)>0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{-x}(x+1)<0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow(x+1)<0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow x<-1 \\ & \therefore \quad x \in(-\infty,-1) \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ The function is increasing in $(-\infty,-1)$.

$\therefore \quad$ For $\mathrm{f}(x)$ to be decreasing, $-\mathrm{e}^{-x}(x+1)<0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{-x}(x+1)>0 \\ & \Rightarrow x+1>0 \\ & \Rightarrow x>-1 \\ & \Rightarrow x \in(-1, \infty) \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ The function is decreasing in $(-1, \infty)$.

content: If the function $\mathrm{f}(x)=x(x+3) \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{x}{2}}$ satisfies all the conditions of Rolle's theorem in $[-3,0]$, then c is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{f}(x) & =x(x+3) \mathrm{e}^{-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) x} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) & =\left(x^2+3 x\right) \mathrm{e}^{-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) x} \cdot\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+(2 x+3) \mathrm{e}^{-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) x} \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) x}\left\{-\frac{1}{2}\left(x^2+3 x\right)+2 x+3\right\} \\ & =-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{e}^{-\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)}\left(x^2-x-6\right) \end{aligned} $$

Since $\mathrm{f}(x)$ satisfies all the conditions of Rolle's theorem, there exists $c \in(-3,0)$ such that

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & f^{\prime}(c)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow c^2-c-6=0 \Rightarrow c=3,-2 \\ & \text { But } c=-2 \in[-3,0] \\ \therefore \quad & c=-2 \end{array} $$

content: A manufacturer produces $x$ items per week at a total cost of ₹ $\left(x^2+78 x+2500\right)$. The price per unit is given by $8 x=600-\mathrm{p}$ where ' p ' is the price of each unit. Then the maximum profit obtained is
explanation:
$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Given, } \\ & \text { Cost price of } x \text { items }=x^2+78 x+2500 \\ & \text { Price per unit }(\mathrm{p})=600-8 x \\ & \text { Selling price of } x \text { items }=(600-8 x) x \\ & \text { Now, } \\ & \text { Profit } \mathrm{P}(x)=\text { Selling Price }- \text { Cost Price } \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{P}(x)=(600-8 x) x-\left(x^2+78 x+2500\right) \\ & =600 x-8 x^2-x^2-78 x-2500\end{array}$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \mathrm{P}(x)=-9 x^2+522 x-2500 \\ \therefore & \mathrm{P}^{\prime}(x)=-18 x+522 \\ & \text { For maxima, } \mathrm{P}^{\prime}(x)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-18 x+522=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{522}{18} \\ & \Rightarrow x=29 \\ & \mathrm{P}^{\prime \prime}(x)=-18<0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{P} \text { is maximum at } x=29 \\ \therefore & \text { Maximum profit } \\ & =-9(29)^2+522(29)-2500 \\ & =5069 \end{array} $$

content: If $2 \mathrm{f}(x)+3 \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=x^2+1, x \neq 0$ and $y=5 x^2 \mathrm{f}(x)$, then $y$ is strictly increasing in
explanation:
$$ 2 \mathrm{f}(x)+3 \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=x^2+1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Replacing $x$ by $\frac{1}{x}$, we get

$$ 2 f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+3 f(x)=\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)^2+1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)$$

Multiplying equation (i) by 2 and equation (ii) by 3 , and then subtracting, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 5 \mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{3}{x^2}+3-2 x^2-2 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{-2 x^2+\frac{3}{x^2}+1}{5} \\ & y=5 x^2 \mathrm{f}(x) \\ & \Rightarrow y=5 x^2\left(\frac{-2 x^2+\frac{3}{x^2}+1}{5}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y=-2 x^4+x^2+3 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-8 x^3+2 x $$

$y$ is strictly increasing

$$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}>0 \\ \Rightarrow & -8 x^3+2 x>0 \\ \Rightarrow & x\left(1-4 x^2\right)>0 \\ \Rightarrow & x>0 \text { and } 1-4 x^2>0 \text { OR } \\ & x<0 \text { and } 1-4 x^2<0 \end{aligned} $$

Case I: $x>0$ and $1-4 x^2>0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x>0 \text { and }|x|<\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow 0

Case II: $x<0$ and $1-4 x^2<0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x<0 \text { and }|x|>\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow x<-\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad y$ is strictly increasing when

$$ x \in\left(-\infty,-\frac{1}{2}\right) \cup\left(0, \frac{1}{2}\right) . $$

content: If the curve $y=a x^2-6 x+b$ passes through $(0,4)$ and has its tangent parallel to the X-axis at $x=\frac{3}{2}$, then the values of $a$ and $b$ respectively are
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \text { Given, } y=a x^2-6 x+b \\ \therefore & \frac{d y}{d x}=2 a x-6 \\ \therefore & \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{\left(x=\frac{3}{2}\right)}=2 a\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)-6=3 a-6 \end{array} $$

Since the tangent is parallel to X -axis at $x=\frac{3}{2}$.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{\left(x=\frac{3}{2}\right)}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 a-6=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 a=6 \\ & \Rightarrow a=2 \end{aligned} $$

Curve $y=a x^2-6 x+b$ passes through $(0,4)$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 4=2(0)^2-6(0)+b \\ & \Rightarrow b=4 \end{aligned} $$

content: 20 is divided into two parts so that the product of the cube of one part and the square of the other part is maximum, then these two parts are
explanation:
Let $x+y=20 \Rightarrow y=20-x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$

and $x^3 y^2=\mathrm{z}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow z=x^3(20-x)^2 \Rightarrow z=400 x^3+x^5-40 x^4 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d z}{d x}=1200 x^2+5 x^4-160 x^3 \end{aligned} $$

For maximum or minimum,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{dz}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1200 x^2+5 x^4-160 x^3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=12,20 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 \mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=2400 x+20 x^3-480 x^2 \\ \therefore \quad & \left(\frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 \mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}\right)_{x=12}=-5760<0 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{z}$ is maximum at $x=12$.

From (i), $y=20-12=8$

$\therefore \quad$ The parts of 20 are 12 and 8.

content: The shortest distance between the line $y-x=1$ and the curve $x=y^2$ is
explanation:
Given equation of line is

$$ x-y+1=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

$$ \therefore \quad \text { Slope }=1 $$

$$ x=y^2 $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ 1=2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 y}\\ &\text { is same as the slope of (i) }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{1}{2 y}=1 \\ \therefore & y=\frac{1}{2} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{P}(x, y)=\left(\frac{1}{4}, \frac{1}{2}\right) \\ \therefore & \text { Shortest distance }=\frac{\left|\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{2}+1\right|}{\sqrt{1+1}}=\frac{3 \sqrt{2}}{8} \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

content: If the curves $y^2=6 x$ and $9 x^2+b y^2=16$ intersect each other at right angles, then the value of $b$ is
explanation:
Let the given curves be:

Curve 1: $ y^2 = 6x $

Curve 2: $ 9x^2 + b y^2 = 16 $

Suppose they intersect at point $ (x_1, y_1) $.

Step 1: Find slopes of the tangents at the point of intersection

For Curve 1:

Differentiate $ y^2 = 6x $ w.r.t $ x $:

$ 2y \frac{dy}{dx} = 6 \implies \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{6}{2y} = \frac{3}{y} $

For Curve 2:

Differentiate $ 9x^2 + b y^2 = 16 $:

$ 18x + 2b y \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 $

$ 2b y \frac{dy}{dx} = -18x \implies \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{-18x}{2b y} = \frac{-9x}{b y} $

Step 2: Condition for orthogonality

The tangents at the intersection are orthogonal, so

$ m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1 $

where

$ m_1 = \frac{3}{y_1}, \quad m_2 = \frac{-9x_1}{b y_1} $

$ \frac{3}{y_1} \cdot \frac{-9x_1}{b y_1} = -1 $

$ \frac{-27x_1}{b y_1^2} = -1 \implies \frac{27x_1}{b y_1^2} = 1 \implies 27 x_1 = b y_1^2 $

Step 3: Use the intersection point

Since the point lies on both curves:

From Curve 1: $ y_1^2 = 6 x_1 $

From above: $ y_1^2 = \frac{27}{b} x_1 $

Set the values of $ y_1^2 $ equal:

$ 6x_1 = \frac{27}{b} x_1 \implies 6 = \frac{27}{b} \implies b = \frac{27}{6} = \frac{9}{2} $

Final Answer:

$ \boxed{\frac{9}{2}} $

So the correct option is Option D: $ \frac{9}{2} $.

content: The position of a point in time $t$ is given by $x=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{bt}-\mathrm{ct}^2, y=\mathrm{at}+\mathrm{bt}^2$. It's resultant acceleration at time $t$ in seconds is given by
explanation:
We are asked to find the size of the acceleration (resultant acceleration) for a point that changes its position with time.

Step 1: Write the formulas for position.
The position in the $x$ direction is $x = \mathrm{a} + \mathrm{bt} - \mathrm{ct}^2$.
The position in the $y$ direction is $y = \mathrm{at} + \mathrm{bt}^2$.

Step 2: Find the second derivatives (acceleration components).
To get acceleration, find the second derivative (differentiation twice) of $x$ and $y$ with respect to $t$.

For $x$:
$$ \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} = \mathrm{b} - 2\mathrm{c}t $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 x}{\mathrm{dt}^2} = -2\mathrm{c} $$

For $y$:
$$ \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}} = \mathrm{a} + 2\mathrm{b}t $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{dt}^2} = 2\mathrm{b} $$

Step 3: Write the acceleration vector.
The acceleration vector $\vec{a} = (-2c, 2b)$.

Step 4: Calculate the magnitude of acceleration.
To get the actual (resultant) acceleration, use the magnitude formula for a vector: $$ |\vec{a}| = \sqrt{(-2c)^2 + (2b)^2} $$ $$ = \sqrt{4c^2 + 4b^2} $$ $$ = 2\sqrt{b^2 + c^2} $$

So, the magnitude of the acceleration is $2\sqrt{b^2 + c^2}$ unit/seconds$^2$.

content: The equation of tangent to the curve $y=\cos (x+y)$ where $-2 \pi \leq x \leq 2 \pi$ and which is parallel to the line $x+2 y=0$, is
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & y=\cos (x+y) \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-\sin (x+y) \cdot\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right) \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-\sin (x+y)-\sin (x+y) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ \therefore & (1+\sin (x+y)) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-\sin (x+y) \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-\sin (x+y)}{1+\sin (x+y)} \end{array} $$

Slope of line $x+2 y=0$ is $\frac{-1}{2}$.

Since the tangent is parallel to $x+2 y=0$.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \frac{-1}{2}=\frac{-\sin (x+y)}{1+\sin (x+y)} \\ & \Rightarrow 1+\sin (x+y)=2 \sin (x+y) \\ & \Rightarrow \sin (x+y)=1 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow y=\cos (x+y)=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \text { point } \mathrm{P}=\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, 0\right) \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Equation of tangent at }\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, 0\right) \text { is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & y-0=\frac{-1}{2}\left(x-\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 2 y=-x+\frac{\pi}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow 4 y=-2 x+\pi \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x+4 y-\pi=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: If two curves $x^2-4 y^2=2$ and $8 x^2=40-\mathrm{m} y^2$ are orthogonal to each other then $\mathrm{m}=$
explanation:
$$ x^2-4 y^2=2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 x-8 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{x}{4 y}=\mathrm{m}_1 \\ & 8 x^2=40-\mathrm{m} y^2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 16 x=-2 m y \frac{d y}{d x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-8 x}{m y}=m_2 \end{aligned} $$

The given curves are orthogonal to each other.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & m_1 \times m_2=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{x}{4 y}\right) \times\left(\frac{-8 x}{m y}\right)=-1 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{2 x^2}{m y^2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2=\frac{m y^2}{2} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii) \end{aligned} $$

Substituting (iii) in equation (i), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 4 y^2=\frac{m y^2}{2}-2 \\ & \Rightarrow 8 y^2=m y^2-4 \\ & \Rightarrow(8-m) y^2=-4 \\ & \Rightarrow y^2=\frac{4}{m-8} \end{aligned} $$

Substituting (iii) in equation (ii), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & y^2=\frac{8}{m} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{4}{m-8}=\frac{8}{m} \\ \Rightarrow & 4 m=8 m-64 \\ \Rightarrow & 64=4 m \\ \Rightarrow & m=16 \end{aligned} $$

content: A population $p(t)$ of 1000 bacteria introduced into a nutrient medium grows according to the relation $\mathrm{p}(\mathrm{t})=1000+\frac{1000 \mathrm{t}}{100+\mathrm{t}^2}$. The maximum size of this bacterial population is
explanation:
Given:

$$ p(t) = 1000 + \frac{1000t}{100 + t^2} $$

To find the maximum size of the population, we need to find the maximum value of $p(t)$ for $t \geq 0$.

Step 1: Find critical points by differentiating $p(t)$

First, differentiate $p(t)$ with respect to $t$:

Let

$$ f(t) = \frac{1000t}{100 + t^2} $$

So,

$$ p'(t) = 0 + \frac{d}{dt} \left( \frac{1000t}{100 + t^2} \right) $$

Using quotient rule:

$$ \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{u}{v}\right) = \frac{v \frac{du}{dt} - u \frac{dv}{dt}}{v^2} $$

where $u = 1000t$, $v = 100 + t^2$.

$$ \frac{du}{dt} = 1000, \quad \frac{dv}{dt} = 2t $$

So,

$$ p'(t) = \frac{(100 + t^2)\cdot 1000 - 1000t \cdot 2t}{(100 + t^2)^2} $$

Simplifying numerator:

$$ (100 + t^2)1000 - 2000t^2 = 1000(100 + t^2 - 2t^2) = 1000(100 - t^2) $$

So,

$$ p'(t) = \frac{1000(100 - t^2)}{(100 + t^2)^2} $$

Step 2: Set $p'(t) = 0$ and solve for $t$:

$$ 1000(100 - t^2) = 0 \implies t^2 = 100 \implies t = 10 \quad (\text{since } t \geq 0) $$

Step 3: Check endpoints and critical point

For $t \to 0$,

$$ p(0) = 1000 + \frac{0}{100} = 1000 $$

For $t = 10$,

$$ p(10) = 1000 + \frac{1000 \cdot 10}{100 + 10^2} = 1000 + \frac{10000}{200} = 1000 + 50 = 1050 $$

For $t \to \infty$,

$$ p(\infty) = 1000 + \frac{1000 \cdot \infty}{100 + \infty^2} = 1000 + \frac{\infty}{\infty} $$

The degree of numerator and denominator is the same (both degree 1 in $t$ in numerator and degree 2 in denominator), so for large $t$:

$$ \frac{1000t}{t^2} = \frac{1000}{t} \to 0 $$

So,

$$ p(\infty) \to 1000 $$

Step 4: Conclusion

Maximum value is at $t = 10$

So, the maximum size is:

$$ \boxed{1050} $$

Option C is correct.

content: By dropping a stone in a quiet lake, a wave in the form of circle is generated. The radius of the circular wave increases at the rate of $2.1 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec}$. Then the rate of increase of the enclosed circular region, when the radius of the circular wave is 10 cm , is (Given $\pi=\frac{22}{7}$)
explanation:
Let the radius of the circle at time $t$ be $r$ (in cm), and let the area of the circle be $A$ (in cm²).

Step 1: Write the formula for area of a circle

$$ A = \pi r^2 $$

Step 2: Differentiate both sides with respect to time $t$

By the chain rule:

$$ \frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(\pi r^2) = \pi \cdot 2r \cdot \frac{dr}{dt} $$

So,

$$ \frac{dA}{dt} = 2\pi r \frac{dr}{dt} $$

Step 3: Substitute the given values

  • $\frac{dr}{dt} = 2.1~\mathrm{cm}/\mathrm{sec}$

  • $r = 10~\mathrm{cm}$

  • $\pi = \frac{22}{7}$

Substitute into the formula:

$ \frac{dA}{dt} = 2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 10 \times 2.1 $

Step 4: Simplify

First multiply $2 \times 10 \times 2.1 = 42$:

$ \frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{22}{7} \times 42 $

Calculate $42 = 6 \times 7$, so

$ \frac{22}{7} \times 42 = 22 \times 6 = 132 $

Step 5: Conclusion

The rate of increase of the enclosed circular region is

$ \boxed{132~\mathrm{cm}^2/\text{second}} $

Correct Option: C

content: The angle between the curves $x y=6$ and $x^2 y=12$ is
explanation:
Solving $x y=6$ and $x^2 y=12$, we get $x=2, y=3$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \text { Consider, } x y=6 \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{6}{x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-6}{x^2} \\ & \mathrm{~m}_1=\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{\mathrm{at} x=2}=\frac{-6}{4}=\frac{-3}{2} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Now, } x^2 y=12 \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{12}{x^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{12(-2)}{x^3}=\frac{-24}{x^3} \\ & \mathrm{~m}_2=\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x_{\text {at } x=2}}=\frac{-24}{8}=-3 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Using, } \tan \theta=\left|\frac{m_1-m_2}{1+m_1 \cdot m_2}\right| \\ & \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \theta & =\tan ^{-1}\left|\frac{m_1-m_2}{1+m_1 \cdot m_2}\right| \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left|\frac{\frac{-3}{2}+3}{1+\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)(-3)}\right| \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{11}\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: In the mean value theorem, $f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}$, if $\mathrm{a}=0, \mathrm{~b}=\frac{1}{2}$ and $\mathrm{f}(x)=x(x-1)(x-2)$, then the value of $c$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} f(x) & =x(x-1)(x-2) \\ f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}-2\right) \\ & =\frac{3}{8} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &f(0)=0\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Using LMVT }\\ &\begin{aligned} f^{\prime}(c) & =\frac{f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)-f(0)}{\frac{1}{2}-0} \\ & =\frac{\frac{3}{8}-0}{\frac{1}{2}} \\ & =\frac{3}{4} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Now, } \mathrm{f}(x)=x(x-1)(x-2) \\ & =x^3-3 x^2+2 x \\ & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2-6 x+2 \\ & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{c})=3 \mathrm{c}^2+6 \mathrm{c}+2 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{c}^2-6 \mathrm{c}+2=\frac{3}{4} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 3 c^2-6 c+\frac{5}{4}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow c=\frac{6 \pm \sqrt{36-15}}{6}=\frac{6 \pm \sqrt{21}}{6}=1 \pm \frac{\sqrt{21}}{6} \\ & c \in\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0\right) \\ \therefore \quad & c=1-\frac{\sqrt{21}}{6} \end{aligned}$$

content: The rate of change of the volume of a sphere with respect to its surface area, when its radius is 2 cm , is _________ $\mathrm{cm}^3 / \mathrm{cm}^2$.
explanation:
Volume of sphere $(V)=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3$

Surface area of sphere $(A)=4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{d V}{d r}=4 \pi r^2 \text { and } \frac{d A}{d r}=8 \pi r \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d V}{d A}=\frac{\frac{d V}{d r}}{\frac{d A}{d r}}=\frac{4 \pi r^2}{8 \pi r}=\frac{r}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad\left(\frac{d V}{d A}\right)_{r=2}=\frac{2}{2}=1 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{cm}^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: Water is being poured at the rate of $36 \mathrm{~m}^3 / \mathrm{min}$ into a cylindrical vessel, whose circular base is of radius 3 meters. Then the water level in the cylinder is rising at the rate of
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dt}}=36 \mathrm{~m}^3 / \mathrm{min}, \text { radius }(\mathrm{r})=3 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \text { Volume }(\mathrm{V})=\pi \mathrm{r}^2 \mathrm{~h} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\pi \mathrm{r}^2 \frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ \Rightarrow & 36=\pi \times(3)^2 \frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{4}{\pi} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{min} \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the normal to the curve $y=x \log x$ parallel to $2 x-2 y+3=0$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{gathered} y=x \log x \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=1+\log x \end{gathered}$$

Slope of the normal $=\frac{-1}{\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}}=-\frac{1}{1+\log x}$

Slope of the given line is 1.

Since the normal is parallel to the given line.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & -\frac{1}{1+\log x}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \log x=-2 \\ & \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{e}^{-2} \\ & y=-2 \mathrm{e}^{-2}\quad\text{... [From (i)]} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of the normal at $\left(\mathrm{e}^{-2},-2 \mathrm{e}^{-2}\right)$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & y+2 \mathrm{e}^{-2}=1\left(x-\mathrm{e}^{-2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y+2 \mathrm{e}^{-2}=x-\mathrm{e}^{-2} \\ & \Rightarrow x-y=3 \mathrm{e}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $x=-1$ and $x=2$ are extreme points of $f(x)=\alpha \log |x|+\beta x^2+x$, then
explanation:
According to the given condition,

$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(-1)=0$ and $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(2)=0$

$f(x)=\alpha \log |x|+\beta x^2+x$

$\therefore \quad f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\alpha}{x}+2 \beta x+1$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(-1)=0 \Rightarrow \alpha+2 \beta=1\quad\text{.... (i)}$

and $f^{\prime}(2)=0 \Rightarrow \alpha+8 \beta=-2\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

From (i) and (ii), we get

$\alpha=2$ and $\beta=\frac{-1}{2}$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(1)=1, \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=3$, then the derivative of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x)))+(\mathrm{f}(x))^2$ at $x=1$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } y=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x)))+(\mathrm{f}(x))^2 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x))) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(x)) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)+2 \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) \\ & \begin{aligned} \left.\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right|_{x=1} & =\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(1))) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{f}(1)) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+2 \mathrm{f}(1) \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1) \\ & =3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3+2 \cdot 1 \cdot 3 \\ & =33 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\theta$ denotes the acute angle between the curves $y=10-x^2$ and $y=2+x^2$, at a point of the intersection, then $|\tan \theta|$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y=10-x^2 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & y=2+x^2\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving (i) and (ii), we get

$$y=6$$

From (i), $6=10-x^2 \Rightarrow x= \pm 2$

Differentiating (i) w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-2 x \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{(-2,6)}=4$$

Differentiating (ii) w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}=2 x \Rightarrow\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(-2,6)}=-4 \\ \therefore\quad& \tan \theta=\left|\frac{4-(-4)}{1+4(-4)}\right|=\left|\frac{8}{-15}\right| \Rightarrow|\tan \theta|=\frac{8}{15} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y=a \log x+b x^2+x$ has its extremum values at $x=-1$ and $x=2$, then
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & y=\mathrm{a} \log x+b x^2+x \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{x}+2 \mathrm{~b} x+1 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=-1}=-\mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b}+1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}+2 \mathrm{~b}=1 \quad\text{....
