(ii)} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Solving (i) and (ii), we get }\\ &a=2, b=-\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: The set of all points, for which $f(x)=x^2 e^{-x}$ strictly increases, is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=2 x \mathrm{e}^{-x}-x^2 \mathrm{e}^{-x}=x \mathrm{e}^{-x}(2-x)\\ &\text { Since } \mathrm{f} \text { is increasing, } \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)>0\\ &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x \mathrm{e}^{-x}(2-x)>0 \\ & \Rightarrow x(2-x)>0 \\ & \Rightarrow 0< x<2 \\ & \Rightarrow x \in(0,2) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The abscissa of the point on the curve $y=\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{a}}+\mathrm{e}^{-\frac{x}{a}}\right)$ where the tangent is parallel to the X -axis is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & y=\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{2}}+\mathrm{e}^{\frac{-x}{2}}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{2}}-\mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{2}} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Since the tangent is parallel to } \mathrm{X} \text {-axis, } \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0\\ &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{2}}-\mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{2}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{2 x}{2}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The Number of values of C that satisfy the conclusion of Rolle's theorem in case of following function $\mathrm{f}(x)=\sin 2 \pi x, x \in[-1,1]$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=\sin 2 \pi x \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=2 \pi \cos 2 \pi x \\ & \text { Now, } \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{C})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \pi \cos 2 \pi \mathrm{C}=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \cos 2 \pi \mathrm{C}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \pi \mathrm{C}=\frac{-3 \pi}{2}, \frac{-\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2} \\ & \quad \ldots[\because x \in(-1,1) \Rightarrow 2 \pi x \in(-2 \pi, 2 \pi)] \\ & \Rightarrow C=\frac{-3}{4},-\frac{1}{4}, \frac{1}{4}, \frac{3}{4} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Number of values of } \mathrm{C}=4 \end{aligned}$$

content: An open tank with a square bottom, to contain 4000 cubic cm . of liquid, is to be constructed. The dimensions of the tank, so that the surface area of the tank is minimum, are
explanation:
Let $x$ be the length of the side of square bottom, h be the height, V be the volume and A be the surface area of open tank.

Then,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}=x^2 \mathrm{~h}=4000 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \mathrm{~A}=x^2+4 x \mathrm{~h}\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

From (i),

$$\mathrm{h}=\frac{4000}{x^2}$$

Substituting the value of $h$ in (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}=x^2+\frac{16000}{x} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2 x-\frac{16000}{x^2} \end{aligned}$$

$A$ is minimum, if $\frac{d A}{d x}=0$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 2 x-\frac{16000}{x^2}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^3=8000 \\ & \Rightarrow x=20 \end{aligned}$$

Now, $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{dx^2}}=2+\frac{32000}{x^3}$

$$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)_{x=20}=6>0$$

$\therefore \quad$ A is minimum when $x=20 \mathrm{~cm}$.

$$\mathrm{h}=\frac{4000}{x^2}=\frac{4000}{400}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$$

content: The function $\mathrm{f}(x)=2 x^3-9 x^2+12 x+2$ is decreasing in
explanation:
To determine where the function is decreasing, we need to find the derivative of the function and analyze where the derivative is negative.

First, compute the derivative of the function:

$$ f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 2 $$

The derivative is:

$$ f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 2) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12 $$

Next, solve the inequality $ f'(x) < 0 $ to determine where the function is decreasing.

Set the derivative equal to zero to find the critical points:

$$ 6x^2 - 18x + 12 = 0 $$

Divide by 6:

$$ x^2 - 3x + 2 = 0 $$

Factor the quadratic equation:

$$ (x - 1)(x - 2) = 0 $$

Thus, the critical points are $ x = 1 $ and $ x = 2 $.

To determine where the derivative is negative, test the intervals created by these critical points: $ x < 1 $, $ 1 < x < 2 $, and $ x > 2 $.

For $ x < 1 $, select $ x = 0 $:

$$ f'(0) = 6(0)^2 - 18(0) + 12 = 12 > 0 $$

For $ 1 < x < 2 $, select $ x = 1.5 $:

$$ f'(1.5) = 6(1.5)^2 - 18(1.5) + 12 = 6\cdot2.25 - 27 + 12 = 13.5 - 27 + 12 = -1.5 < 0 $$

For $ x > 2 $, select $ x = 3 $:

$$ f'(3) = 6(3)^2 - 18(3) + 12 = 54 - 54 + 12 = 12 > 0 $$

Therefore, the function is decreasing in the interval:

Option A: $ 1 < x < 2 $

content: The equation of motion of a particle is $s=a t^2+b t+c$. If the displacement after 1 second is 20 m , velocity after 2 seconds is $30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$ and the acceleration is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}^2$, then
explanation:
$$s=a t^2+b t+c$$

Displacement after 1 second is 20 m.

$$\therefore \quad 20=a+b+c\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

$$\text { Velocity }=\mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{ds}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{at}+\mathrm{b}$$

Velocity after 2 seconds is $30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$

$$\therefore \quad 30=4 a+b\quad\text{.... (ii)}$$

Acceleration $=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{a}$

Acceleration is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}^2$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & 10=2 a \\ & \Rightarrow a=5 \end{aligned}$$

From (ii), we get $\mathrm{b=10}$

From (i), we get $\mathrm{c}=5$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}=\mathrm{b}$$

content: After $t$ seconds, the acceleration of a particle, which starts from rest and moves in a straight line is $\left(8-\frac{\mathrm{t}}{5}\right) \mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}^2$, then velocity of the particle at the instant, when the acceleration is zero, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Acceleration }=\left(8-\frac{\mathrm{t}}{5}\right) \mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=8-\frac{\mathrm{t}}{5} \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$v=8 t-\frac{t^2}{10}+c\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

At $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{v}=0$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 0=8(0)-0+c \Rightarrow c=0 \\ \therefore & v=8 t-\frac{t^2}{10}\quad\text{.... (i) [From (i)]} \end{array}$$

Acceleration $=0$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 8-\frac{\mathrm{t}}{5}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=40 \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $\mathrm{t}=40$ in (ii), we get

Velocity $(v)=8(40)-\frac{(40)^2}{10}=160 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=x^3-10 x^2+200 x-10$, then

options.content: $\mathrm{f}(x)$ is decreasing in $(-\infty, 10]$ and increasing in $[10, \infty)$
$f(x)$ is increasing in $(-\infty, 10]$ and decreasing in $[10, \infty)$

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=x^3-10 x^2+200 x-10 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2-20 x+200 \end{aligned}$$

For $\mathrm{f}(x)$ to be increasing $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)>0$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 3 x^2-20 x+200>0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3\left(x^2-\frac{20}{3} x+\frac{200}{3}+\frac{100}{9}-\frac{100}{9}\right)>0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3\left[\left(x-\frac{10}{3}\right)^2+\frac{500}{9}\right]>0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3\left(x-\frac{10}{3}\right)^2+\frac{500}{3}>0 \end{aligned}$$

Always increasing throughout real line.

content: Twenty meters of wire is available for fencing off a flower-bed in the form of a circular sector. Then the maximum area (in sq.m) of the flowerbed is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Total length of wire }=r+r+r \theta\\ &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 20=2 \mathrm{r}+\mathrm{r} \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{20-2 \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \mathrm{~A}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{r}^2 \theta \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{r}^2\left(\frac{20-2 \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}\right)=10 \mathrm{r}-\mathrm{r}^2 \\ \therefore\quad& \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{~d}}=10-2 \mathrm{r} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

For maximum area, $\frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dr}}=0$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 10-2 \mathrm{r}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=5 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{dr}^2}=-2<0 \end{aligned}$$

Area is maximum at $\mathrm{r}=5$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Maximum area } & =10(5)-5^2 \\ & =50-25=25 \mathrm{~sq} . \mathrm{m} \end{aligned}$$

content: A ladder 5 m in length is leaning against a wall. The bottom of the ladder is pulled along the ground away from the wall, at the rate of $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$. How fast is the height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 4 m away from the wall?
explanation:
In right angled $\triangle A O C$,

$$\begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2=(5)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow y^2=5^2-x^2 \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. t, we get

$$\begin{aligned} 2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{dt}} & =-2 x \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{dt}} & =-\frac{x}{y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & =\frac{-x}{\sqrt{5^2-x^2}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad\left(\frac{d y}{d t}\right)_{x=4} & =\frac{-4}{\sqrt{25-16}} \cdot(2) \\ & =\frac{-8}{3} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, the height on the wall is decreasing at the rate of $\frac{8}{3} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$

content: If $y=\mathrm{a} \log x+\mathrm{b} x^2+x$ has its extreme values at $x=-1$ and $x=2$, then the value of $\left(\frac{a}{b}+\frac{b}{a}\right)$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{x}+2 \mathrm{~b} x+1 \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=-1}=-\mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b}+1= 0\\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=-2 \mathrm{~b}+1 \\ & \text { and }\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{x=2}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}+4 \mathrm{~b}+1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-2 \mathrm{~b}+1}{2}+4 \mathrm{~b}+1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-\mathrm{b}+4 \mathrm{~b}+\frac{3}{2}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~b}=\frac{-3}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}=\frac{-1}{2} \text { and } \mathrm{a}=2 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{~b}}+\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}\right)=\frac{-17}{4} \end{aligned}$$

content: The curve $y=a x^3+b x^2+c x+5$ touches the X - axis at $(-2,0)$ and cuts the Y -axis at a point Q where its gradient is 3 , then values of $a, b, c$ respectively, are
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y=a x^3+b x^2+c x+5 \text { touches X-axis at }(-2,0) \\\\ & \Rightarrow 0=-8 a+4 b-2 c+5 \\\\ & \Rightarrow 8 a-4 b+2 c=5\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Also, it cuts Y -axis at a point Q

$\therefore$ Put $x=0$ in the equation of curve, we get

$$y=5$$

$\therefore \mathrm{Q} \equiv(0,5)$

$$y=a x^3+b x^2+c x+5$$

$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=3 \mathrm{a} x^2+2 \mathrm{~b} x+\mathrm{c}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{\mathrm{Q}(0.5)}=3 \\\\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{a}(0)^2+2 \mathrm{~b}(0)+\mathrm{c}=3 \\\\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=3 \end{aligned}$$

Equation (i) becomes,

$$\begin{aligned} & 8 a-4 b+6=5 \\\\ & \Rightarrow 8 a-4 b+1=0\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Option (C) satisfies equation (ii)

$\therefore$ Option (C) is correct

content: The maximum value of the function $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=2 \mathrm{x}^3-15 x^2+36 x-48$ on the set $A=\left\{x / x^2+20 \leq 9 x\right\}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Let } \mathrm{f}(x)=2 x^3-15 x^2+36 x-48 \\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=6 x^2-30 x+36=0 \text { at } x=3,2 \\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(x)=12 x-30 \\ & \mathrm{~A}=\left\{x \mid x^2-9 x+20 \leq 0\right\}=[4,5] \\ \therefore & 2,3 \notin \mathrm{~A} \\ \therefore & \text { At } x=4, \mathrm{f}(x)=-16 \text { and at } x=5, \mathrm{f}(x)=7 \\ \therefore & \text { Maximum value of } \mathrm{f}(x) \text { is at } x=5 \\ \therefore & \text { Maximum value of } \mathrm{f}(x) \text { is } 7 . \end{array}$$

content: If the normal to the curve $y=\mathrm{f}(x)$ at the point $(3,4)$ makes an angle of $\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)$ with the positive $X$-axis, then the value of $f^{\prime}(3)$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Slope of the normal }=\frac{-1}{\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \frac{3 \pi}{4}=\frac{-1}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{(3,4)}} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{(3,4)}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(3)=1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=x^3-6 x^2+9 x+3$ is monotonically decreasing function, then $x$ lies in
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & f(x)=x^3-6 x^2+9 x+3 \\ \therefore \quad & f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2-12 x+9 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Since } f(x) \text { is monotonically decreasing, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)<0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 x^2-12 x+9<0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2-4 x+3<0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-3)(x-1)<0 \\ & \Rightarrow x \in(1,3) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If equation of normal to the curve $x=\sqrt{t}$, $y=\mathrm{t}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{t}}}$ at $\mathrm{t}=4$ is
explanation:
$$x=\sqrt{\mathrm{t}}, y=\mathrm{t}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{t}}}$$

$\therefore \quad y=x^2-\frac{1}{x}$, at $\mathrm{t}=4, x=2$ and $y=\frac{7}{2}$

$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 x+\frac{1}{x^2}$

$\therefore \quad$ Slope of the normal at $\mathrm{t}=4$ is $\frac{-1}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{\mathrm{t}=4}}=\frac{-4}{17}$

$\therefore \quad$ Required equation is $\left(y-\frac{7}{2}\right)=\frac{-4}{17}(x-2)$

i.e., $8 x+34 y=135$

content: The rate of change of the volume of a sphere with respect to its surface area, when its radius is 2 cm , is
explanation:
Volume of sphere $(V)=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3$

Surface area of sphere $(A)=4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2$

$\frac{d V}{d r}=4 \pi r^2$ and $\frac{d A}{d r}=8 \pi r$

$\begin{aligned} & \therefore\left(\frac{d V}{d A}\right)=\frac{\left(\frac{d V}{d r}\right)}{\left(\frac{d A}{d r}\right)}=\frac{4 \pi r^2}{8 \pi r}=\frac{r}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad\left(\frac{d V}{d A}\right)_{r=2}=1 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{cm}^2 \end{aligned}$

content: The distance ' $s$ ' in meters covered by a body in $t$ seconds is given by $s=3 t^2-8 t+5$. The body will stop after
explanation:
To determine when the body stops, we need to find when the velocity of the body becomes zero. The velocity is the derivative of the distance function with respect to time.

Given the distance function:

$$ s(t) = 3t^2 - 8t + 5 $$

The velocity function, $ v(t) $, is the derivative of $ s(t) $:

$$ v(t) = \frac{ds}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} (3t^2 - 8t + 5) $$

Calculating the derivative:

$$ v(t) = 6t - 8 $$

Set the velocity to zero to find when the body stops:

$$ 6t - 8 = 0 $$

Solve for $ t $:

$$ 6t = 8 $$

$$ t = \frac{8}{6} $$

$$ t = \frac{4}{3} $$

Thus, the body will stop after $\frac{4}{3}$ seconds.

Option C is the correct answer.

content: The volume of a ball is increasing at the rate of $4 \pi \mathrm{cc} / \mathrm{sec}$. The rate of increase of the radius, when the volume is $288 \pi \mathrm{cc}$, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3 \\ & \Rightarrow 288 \pi=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=6 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \\ & \frac{d V}{d t}=4 \pi r^2 \frac{d r}{d t} \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \pi=4 \pi r^2 \frac{d r}{d t} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d r}{d t}=\frac{1}{r^2}=\frac{1}{36} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\mathrm{f}(x)=(x-1)(x-2)(x-3), x \in[0,4]$. Values of C will be __________ if L.M.V.T. (Lagrange's Mean Value Theorem) can be applied.
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } y=(x-1)(x-2)(x-3) \\ & \log y=\log (x-1)+\log (x-2)+\log (x-3) \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{y} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1}{x-1}+\frac{1}{x-2}+\frac{1}{x-3} \\ \therefore\quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=(x-2)(x-3)+(x-1)(x-3) \\ & \quad+(x-1)(x-2) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{c})=\frac{\mathrm{f}(4)-\mathrm{f}(0)}{4-0}=\frac{6-(-6)}{4}=3, \text { for } \mathrm{c} \in[0,4] \\ \therefore & (\mathrm{c}-2)(\mathrm{c}-3)+(\mathrm{c}-1)(\mathrm{c}-3)+(c-1)(c-2)=3 \\ \therefore & 3 \mathrm{c}^2-12 \mathrm{c}+11=3 \\ \therefore & 3 \mathrm{c}^2-12 \mathrm{c}+8=0 \\ \therefore & \mathrm{c}=\frac{12 \pm \sqrt{144-96}}{6}=\frac{6 \pm 2 \sqrt{3}}{3} \end{array}$$

content: If $y=4 x-5$ is a tangent to the curve $y^2=p x^3+q$ at $(2,3)$, then the values of $p$ and $q$ are respectively
explanation:
$$y^2=p x^3+q\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 y \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=3 \mathrm{p} x^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3 \mathrm{p}}{2}\left(\frac{x^2}{y}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & \left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{(2,3)}=\frac{3 \mathrm{p}}{2} \times \frac{4}{3}=2 \mathrm{p} \end{aligned}$$

Since the line touches the curve, their slopes are equal.

$$\therefore \quad 2 p=4 \Rightarrow p=2$$

Since $(2,3)$ lies on $y^2=p x^3+q$.

$$\therefore \quad 9=2 \times 8+q \Rightarrow q=-7$$

content: Water is running in a hemispherical bowl of radius 180 cm at the rate of 108 cubic decimeters per minute. How fast the water level is rising when depth of the water level in the bowl is 120 cm ? ( 1 decimeter $=10 \mathrm{~cm}$)
explanation:
Radius of hemispherical bowl $(\mathrm{r})=180 \mathrm{~cm}$

Rate of flow $\left(\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)=108 \mathrm{dm}^3 / \mathrm{min}$

$$\begin{aligned} & =108000 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{min} \\ & =\frac{108000}{60} \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{sec} \\ & =1800 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned}$$

Let depth of water in bowl be $x$.

$\therefore \quad$ Volume of water in hemispherical

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { bowl }(\mathrm{V})=\frac{\pi}{3} x^2(3 \mathrm{r}-x) \\ & \mathrm{V}=\frac{\pi}{3} x^2(3 \times 180-x) \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{V}=180 \pi x^2-\frac{\pi}{3} x^3 \end{aligned}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{d V}{d t}=360 \pi x \frac{d x}{d t}-\pi x^2 \frac{d x}{d t} \\ & \left.\frac{d V}{d t}\right|_{x=120}=\frac{d x}{d t}\left(360 \pi \times 120-120^2 \pi\right) \\ \therefore\quad& 1800=\frac{d x}{d t}\left(360 \pi \times 120-120^2 \pi\right) \\ \therefore\quad& 15=\frac{d x}{d t}(360 \pi-120 \pi) \\ & \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{15}{240 \pi}=\frac{1}{16 \pi} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: A point moves along the arc of parabola $y=2 x^2$. Its abscissa increases uniformly at the rate of 2 units $/ \mathrm{sec}$. At the instant, the point is passing through ( 1,2 ), its distance from origin is increasing at the rate of
explanation:
Given, $\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=2$ units/sec

Given equation of parabola is $$y=2 x^2$$

Differentiating w.r.to $t$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}=4 x \times \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}=8 x\quad\text { ... (i) }\left[\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2\right] \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ The distance of point from origin is given by $\sqrt{x^2+y^2}$

$\therefore \quad$ The rate of increasing distance of point from origin $=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left(\sqrt{x^2+y^2}\right)$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x^2+y^2}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left(x^2+y^2\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x^2+y^2}} \cdot\left[2 x \times \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}+2 y \times \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x^2+y^2}} \cdot[4 x+2 y \times 8 x] \quad\text { ...[From (i)] }\\ & =\frac{(2 x+8 x y)}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}} \end{aligned}\\ \end{aligned}$$

Since point is passing through $(1,2)$

Rate of increasing distance of point from origin

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{2(1)+8(1)(2)}{\sqrt{1^2+2^2}} \\ & =\frac{18}{\sqrt{5}} \text { units } / \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the normal to the curve $y=x \log x$, which is parallel to the line $2 x-2 y+3=0$, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &y=x \log x\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.to } x \text {, we get }\\ &\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=1+\log x \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore\quad$ Slope of tangent $=1+\log x$

Slope of given line $=1$

$\therefore\quad$ Line of normal is parallel to given line

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow 1+\log x=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow \log x=-2 \\ & \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{e}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $x=\mathrm{e}^{-2}$ in given equation,

$$\begin{aligned} y & =x \log x \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{-2} \log \mathrm{e}^{-2} \\ & =-2 \mathrm{e}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore\quad$ Equation of line passing through $\left(e^{-2},-2 \mathrm{e}^{-2}\right)$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & y+2 \mathrm{e}^{-2}=1\left(x-\mathrm{e}^{-2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow x-y=3 \mathrm{e}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

content: The function $\mathrm{f}(x)=2 x^3-6 x+5$ is an increasing function, if
explanation:
To determine where the function $f(x) = 2x^3 - 6x + 5$ is increasing, we first need to find its derivative:

$$ f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(2x^3 - 6x + 5) = 6x^2 - 6. $$

For the function to be increasing, its derivative must be positive:

$$ 6x^2 - 6 > 0. $$

Simplifying, we have:

$$ 6(x^2 - 1) > 0. $$

Divide both sides by 6:

$$ x^2 - 1 > 0. $$

This can be factored as:

$$ (x - 1)(x + 1) > 0. $$

The critical points are $x = 1$ and $x = -1$. To determine the intervals where the inequality holds, we test intervals around the critical points:

For $x < -1$, choose $x = -2$: $(-2 - 1)(-2 + 1) = (-3)(-1) = 3 > 0$. The inequality holds.

Between $-1 < x < 1$, choose $x = 0$: $(0 - 1)(0 + 1) = (-1)(1) = -1 < 0$. The inequality does not hold.

For $x > 1$, choose $x = 2$: $(2 - 1)(2 + 1) = (1)(3) = 3 > 0$. The inequality holds.

So, the function is increasing on the intervals $x < -1$ and $x > 1$. Thus, the correct interval where the function is increasing is:

Option C: $x < -1$ or $x > 1$.

content: A square plate is contracting at the uniform rate $3 \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{sec}$, then the rate at which the perimeter is decreasing, when the side of the square is 15 cm , is
explanation:
The side length of a square is denoted by $ s $, and the area $ A $ of the square is given by:

$$ A = s^2 $$

Given that the area of the square is contracting at a rate of $ \frac{dA}{dt} = -3 \, \text{cm}^2/\text{sec} $.

Using the chain rule, the rate of change of the area with respect to time is related to the rate of change of the side length as follows:

$$ \frac{dA}{dt} = 2s \frac{ds}{dt} $$

Substitute the given values into this equation:

$$ -3 = 2(15) \frac{ds}{dt} $$

Solving for $ \frac{ds}{dt} $:

$$ -3 = 30 \frac{ds}{dt} $$

$$ \frac{ds}{dt} = -\frac{1}{10} \, \text{cm/sec} $$

The perimeter $ P $ of the square is given by:

$$ P = 4s $$

Differentiating with respect to time $ t $:

$$ \frac{dP}{dt} = 4 \frac{ds}{dt} $$

Substituting the value of $ \frac{ds}{dt} $:

$$ \frac{dP}{dt} = 4 \left(-\frac{1}{10}\right) $$

$$ \frac{dP}{dt} = -\frac{4}{10} \, \text{cm/sec} $$

$$ \frac{dP}{dt} = -\frac{2}{5} \, \text{cm/sec} $$

Thus, the rate at which the perimeter is decreasing is $ \frac{2}{5} \, \text{cm/sec} $. The correct answer is Option B:

$\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec}$

content: A poster is to be printed on a rectangular sheet of paper of area $18 \mathrm{~m}^2$. The margins at the top and bottom of 75 cm each and at the sides 50 cm each are to be left. Then the dimensions i.e. height and breadth of the sheet so that the space available for printing is maximum, are _______ respectively.
explanation:
Let height and breadth of the sheet be ' $y$ ' m and ' $x$ ' m respectively.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & x y=180000 \mathrm{~cm}^2 \\ \therefore & y=\frac{180000}{x} \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad$ The area available for printing is

$$\begin{aligned} A & =(y-150)(x-100) \\ & =\left(\frac{180000}{x}-150\right)(x-100) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =180000-\frac{18000000}{x}-150 x-15000 \\ & =165000-150 x-\frac{18000000}{x} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0-150+\frac{18000000}{x^2} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \Rightarrow x^2=\frac{18000000}{150}=120000 \\ \Rightarrow x & =200 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm} \\ \Rightarrow y & =\frac{180000}{200 \sqrt{3}}=300 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$$

Now, $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{-36000000}{x^3}$

$$\therefore \quad \text { At } x=200 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}, \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{dx}}<0$$

$\therefore$ Area is maximum at $x=200 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ and $y=300 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$

$\therefore \quad y=3 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$ and $x=2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$

content: The equation of the tangent to the curve $x=\operatorname{acos}^3 \theta, y=\operatorname{asin}^3 \theta$ at $\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & x=\operatorname{acos}^3 \theta \text { and } y=a \sin ^3 \theta \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}=-3 \cos ^2 \theta \sin \theta \text { and } \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}=3 a \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}}{\frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}}=-\tan \theta \\ \therefore \quad & \left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{\left(\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}=-1 \\ & \text { At } \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}, \\ & x=\mathrm{a} \cos ^3 \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2 \sqrt{2}} \\ & y=a \sin ^3 \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2 \sqrt{2}} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore\quad&\text { Equation of the tangent at }\left(\frac{a}{2 \sqrt{2}}, \frac{a}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right) \text { is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & y-\frac{a}{2 \sqrt{2}}=-1\left(x-\frac{a}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow x+y=\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let C be a curve given by $y(x)=1+\sqrt{4 x-3}$, $x>\frac{3}{4}$. If P is a point on C , such that the tangent at P has slope $\frac{2}{3}$, then a point through which the normal at P passes, is
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & y(x)=1+\sqrt{4 x-3} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{4}{2 \sqrt{4 x-3}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{4 x-3}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2}{\sqrt{4 x-3}}=\frac{2}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow x=3 \\ \therefore \quad & y=4 \end{array}$$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{\log x}{x}(x>0)$, then it is increasing in
explanation:
To determine where the function $$ f(x) = \frac{\log x}{x} $$ is increasing, we need to examine its derivative and determine the intervals where the derivative is positive.

First, let's find the first derivative of the function using the quotient rule. The quotient rule states:

$$ \left(\frac{u}{v}\right)' = \frac{u'v - uv'}{v^2} $$

where $ u = \log x $ and $ v = x $.

Calculating the derivatives of $ u $ and $ v $:

$ u' = \frac{1}{x} $

$ v' = 1 $

Applying the quotient rule:

$ f'(x) = \frac{\frac{1}{x} \cdot x - \log x \cdot 1}{x^2} = \frac{1 - \log x}{x^2} $

To find where $ f(x) $ is increasing, we set $ f'(x) > 0 $:

$ \frac{1 - \log x}{x^2} > 0 $

Since $ x^2 > 0 $ for $ x > 0 $, the inequality $ 1 - \log x > 0 $ simplifies to:

$ 1 > \log x $

Equivalently,

$ \log x < 1 \implies x < e $

Thus, the function $ f(x) $ is increasing in the interval $ (0, e) $.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A: $ (0, e) $

content: The maximum value of $\frac{\log x}{x}$ is
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{\log x}{x} \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{x^2}-\frac{\log x}{x^2}$

For maximum or minimum value of $\mathrm{f}(x)$,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1-\log _e x}{x^2}=0 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad \log _{\mathrm{e}} x=1$ or $x=\mathrm{e}$, which lie in $(0, \infty)$.

For $x=e, \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=-\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}^3}$, which is $\mathrm{-ve}$.

$\therefore \quad y$ is maximum at $x=\mathrm{e}$ and its maximum value $=\frac{\log e}{e}=\frac{1}{e}$.

content: If the curves $y^2=6 x, 9 x^2+\mathrm{b} y^2=16$ intersect each other at right angles, then the value of $b$ is
explanation:
To solve for the value of $ b $ when the curves intersect at right angles, we first need to determine the derivatives of each curve.

For the first curve $ y^2 = 6x $, we can differentiate implicitly with respect to $ x $:

$ 2y \frac{dy}{dx} = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{3}{y} $

For the second curve $ 9x^2 + b y^2 = 16 $, differentiate implicitly:

$ 18x + 2b y \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{9x}{by} $

At the point of intersection where the curves meet at right angles, the product of their slopes must be $-1$:

$ \left( \frac{3}{y} \right) \left( -\frac{9x}{by} \right) = -1 $

Simplifying, we have:

$ -\frac{27x}{by^2} = -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{27x}{by^2} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad by^2 = 27x $

Since $ y^2 = 6x $ from the first equation, substitute $ y^2 = 6x $ into the equation $ by^2 = 27x $:

$ b(6x) = 27x \quad \Rightarrow \quad 6bx = 27x $

For non-zero $ x $, we divide both sides by $ x $:

$ 6b = 27 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = \frac{27}{6} = \frac{9}{2} $

Thus, the value of $ b $ is:

$ b = \frac{9}{2} $

Hence, the correct answer is option A, $ \frac{9}{2} $.

content: If Mean value theorem holds for the function $\mathrm{f}(x)=(x-1)(x-2)(x-3), x \in[0,4]$ then the values of $c$ as per the theorem are
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=(x-1)(x-2)(x-3) \\ & \mathrm{f}(4)=(4-1)(4-2)(4-3) \\ & \mathrm{f}(4)=6 \\ & \mathrm{f}(0)=(0-1)(0-2)(0-3)=-6 \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Using LMVT }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{c})=\frac{\mathrm{f}(4)-\mathrm{f}(0)}{4-0}=\frac{6-(-6)}{4}=3 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{c})=3 \quad\text{.... (i)} \end{array}\\ &\begin{aligned} \text { Now, } \mathrm{f}(x) & =(x-1)(x-2)(x-3) \\ & =x^3-6 x^2+11 x-6 \end{aligned}\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2-12 x+11 \\ & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{c})=3 \quad\text { ...[From (i)] }\\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{c}^2-12 \mathrm{c}+11=3 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{c}^2-12 \mathrm{c}+8=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\frac{12 \pm \sqrt{48}}{6}=2 \pm \frac{2 \sqrt{3}}{3}=2 \pm \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \end{aligned}\\ \end{aligned}$$

content: A ladder 5 m long rests against a vertical wall. If its top slides downwards at the rate of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec}$., then the foot of the ladder is sliding at the rate of _________ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{sec}$., when it is 4 m away from the wall.
explanation:
According to the figure, $x^2+y^2=25\quad\text{.... (i)}$

At $x=4, y=3$

Differentiating (i) with respect to ' $t$ ', we get

content: The equation of the tangent to the curve $y=1-\mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{3}}$ at the point of intersection with Y -axis is
explanation:
Given equation of curve is

$y=1-\mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{3}}\quad\text{... (i)}$

Since, curve intersects Y-axis, $x=0$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad y=1-\mathrm{e}^{\frac{0}{3}}=1-1 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow y=0 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Tangent to the curve passes through origin

$\therefore \quad$ Slope of tangent $=\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$

$\therefore \quad$ Differentiating (i) w.r.to $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-\mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{3}}}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{(0,0)}=\frac{-\mathrm{e}^{\frac{0}{3}}}{3}=\frac{-1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of tangent is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-0=\frac{-1}{3}(x-0) \\ & \Rightarrow 3 y=-x \\ & \Rightarrow x+3 y=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $8 \mathrm{f}(x)+6 \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=x+5$ and $y=x^2 \mathrm{f}(x)$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ at $x=-1$ is
explanation:
$$8 f(x)+6 f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=x+5\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$8 f^{\prime}(x)+6 f^{\prime}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{-1}{x^2}\right)=1\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

Substituting $x=-1$ in (i), we get

$8 \mathrm{f}(-1)+6 \mathrm{f}(-1)=4$

$\therefore\quad14 \mathrm{f}(-1)=4$

$$\therefore\quad f(-1)=\frac{4}{14}=\frac{2}{7}\quad\text{.... (iii)}$$

Substituting $x=-1$ in (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(-1)=\frac{1}{2} \quad\text{.... (iv)}\\ & y=x^2 \mathrm{f}(x) \\ \therefore\quad & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 x \mathrm{f}(x)+x^2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \left.\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right|_{x=-1} & =2(-1) \mathrm{f}(-1)+(-1)^2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(-1) \\ & =\frac{-4}{7}+\frac{1}{2} \quad \ldots[\text { From (iii) and (iv) }] \\ & =-\frac{1}{14} \end{aligned}$$

content: The function $f(x)=\frac{\log _e(\pi+x)}{\log _e(e+x)}$ is
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{\ln (\pi+x)}{\ln (\mathrm{e}+x)}$

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) & =\frac{\ln (\mathrm{e}+x) \times \frac{1}{\pi+x}-\ln (\pi+x) \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{e}+x}}{[\ln (\mathrm{e}+x)]^2} \\ & =\frac{(\mathrm{e}+x) \ln (\mathrm{e}+x)-(\pi+x) \ln (\pi+x)}{[\ln (\mathrm{e}+x)]^2 \times(\mathrm{e}+x)(\pi+x)} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) & <0 \text { for all } x>0\quad \ldots .[\because \pi>\mathrm{e}] \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x)$ is decreasing on $(0, \infty)$.

content: The sum of intercepts on coordinate axes made by tangent to the curve $\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{y}=\sqrt{a}$ is
explanation:
$$\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{y}=\sqrt{a}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{y}} \frac{d y}{d x}=0$$

$\therefore \quad$ Slope of the tangent is $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\sqrt{\frac{y}{x}}$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of the tangent at $\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ is

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \left(y-y_1\right)=-\sqrt{\frac{y_1}{x_1}}\left(x-x_1\right) \\ & \sqrt{x_1} y+\sqrt{y_1} x=\sqrt{x_1} y_1+\sqrt{y_1} x_1 \\ \therefore \quad & \sqrt{y_1} x+\sqrt{x_1} y=\sqrt{x_1 y_1}\left(\sqrt{x_1}+\sqrt{y_1}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & \sqrt{y_1} x+\sqrt{x_1} y=\sqrt{x_1 y_1 \mathrm{a}} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{x}{\sqrt{x_1 a}}+\frac{y}{\sqrt{y_1 \mathrm{a}}}=1 \\ \therefore \quad & x \text {-intercept }+y \text {-intercept }= \\ & =\sqrt{\mathrm{a}}\left(\sqrt{x_1}+y_1\right) \\ & =\sqrt{\mathrm{a}} \times \sqrt{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{a} \end{array}$$

content: A wire of length 2 units is cut into two parts, which are bent respectively to form a square of side $x$ units and a circle of radius of r units. If the sum of the areas of square and the circle so formed is minimum, then
explanation:
The total length of the wire is 2 units. This wire is cut into two parts:

One part of length $4x$ used to form a square of side $x$.

The other part of length $2\pi r$ used to form a circle of radius $r$.

The perimeter constraint is:

$ 4x + 2\pi r = 2. $

Express one variable in terms of the other:

From the constraint, we can express $x$ in terms of $r$:

$ 4x = 2 - 2\pi r \implies x = \frac{2 - 2\pi r}{4} = \frac{1 - \pi r}{2}. $

Write the area function:

The total area $A$ is the sum of the area of the square and the area of the circle:

$ A = \text{(Area of square)} + \text{(Area of circle)} = x^2 + \pi r^2. $

Substitute $x = \frac{1 - \pi r}{2}$ into the area expression:

$ A = \left(\frac{1 - \pi r}{2}\right)^2 + \pi r^2. $

Expand the square:

$ \left(\frac{1 - \pi r}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{(1 - \pi r)^2}{4} = \frac{1 - 2\pi r + \pi^2 r^2}{4}. $

Thus:

$ A = \frac{1 - 2\pi r + \pi^2 r^2}{4} + \pi r^2. $

Combine like terms:

$ A = \frac{1}{4} - \frac{2\pi r}{4} + \frac{\pi^2 r^2}{4} + \pi r^2. $

Factor where possible:

$ A = \frac{1}{4} - \frac{\pi r}{2} + \left(\frac{\pi^2 r^2}{4} + \pi r^2\right). $

Combine the $r^2$ terms:

$ \frac{\pi^2 r^2}{4} + \pi r^2 = \frac{\pi^2 r^2 + 4\pi r^2}{4} = \frac{\pi(\pi+4)r^2}{4}. $

Hence:

$ A = \frac{1}{4} - \frac{\pi r}{2} + \frac{\pi(\pi+4)r^2}{4}. $

Find the critical point for minimization:

Differentiate $A$ with respect to $r$:

$ A'(r) = -\frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{\pi(\pi+4)}{2}r. $

Set $A'(r) = 0$ to find the critical point:

$ -\frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{\pi(\pi+4)}{2}r = 0. $

Divide through by $\frac{\pi}{2}$ (nonzero):

$ -1 + (\pi+4)r = 0 \implies (\pi+4)r = 1 \implies r = \frac{1}{\pi+4}. $

Find the corresponding $x$:

Substitute $r = \frac{1}{\pi+4}$ into $x = \frac{1-\pi r}{2}$:

$ x = \frac{1 - \pi\left(\frac{1}{\pi+4}\right)}{2} = \frac{1 - \frac{\pi}{\pi+4}}{2} = \frac{\frac{\pi+4-\pi}{\pi+4}}{2} = \frac{4}{(\pi+4) \cdot 2} = \frac{2}{\pi+4}. $

We now have:

$ x = \frac{2}{\pi+4}, \quad r = \frac{1}{\pi+4}. $

Check the given options:

Option A: $2x = (\pi+4)r$

Substitute values:

$ 2x = 2 \cdot \frac{2}{\pi+4} = \frac{4}{\pi+4}, \quad (\pi+4)r = (\pi+4)\cdot \frac{1}{\pi+4} = 1. $

Not equal.

Option B: $(4-\pi)x = \pi r$

Substitute:

$ (4-\pi)x = (4-\pi)\frac{2}{\pi+4}, \quad \pi r = \pi\frac{1}{\pi+4}. $

This does not simplify to a known identity matching our values.

Option C: $x = 2r$

Substitute:

$ 2r = 2\cdot\frac{1}{\pi+4} = \frac{2}{\pi+4}, \quad x = \frac{2}{\pi+4}. $

This matches perfectly. $x = 2r$.

Option D: $2x = r$

Substitute:

$ 2x = 2\cdot\frac{2}{\pi+4} = \frac{4}{\pi+4}, \quad r = \frac{1}{\pi+4}. $

Not equal.

The correct relation is $ x = 2r $.

Final Answer:

$ \boxed{x = 2r} \text{ (Option C)} $

content: If sum of two numbers is 3 , then the maximum value of the product of first number and square of the second number is
explanation:
Let the two numbers be $a$ and $b$.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad a+b=3 \\ \therefore \quad b=3-a\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Product of first number and square of second number $(p)=a b^2$

$$\mathrm{p}=\mathrm{a}(3-\mathrm{a})^2$$ $\ldots[\operatorname{From}(\mathrm{i})]$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad p=9 a-6 a^2+a^3 \quad\text{.... (ii)}\\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d p}{d a}=9-12 a+3 a^2 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d^2 p}{d a^2}=-12+6 a \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { Now, } \frac{d p}{d a}=0 & \Rightarrow 3 a^2-12 a+9=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(3 a-9)(a-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow a=3 \text { or } a=1 \\ \left.\frac{d^2 p}{d a^2}\right|_{a-3}=6 & >0 \text { and }\left.\frac{d^2 p}{d a^2}\right|_{a=1}=-6<0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Maximum value or $p$ is at $a=1$

$\therefore \quad$ Maximum value of the product $=4$ $\ldots[$ From (ii)]

content: The curve $y=a x^3+b x^2+c x+5$ touches the $x$-axis at $(-2,0)$ and cuts the $y$-axis at a point Q where its gradient is 3 , then the value of $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}$ is
explanation:
To solve this problem, it's necessary to apply the conditions given for the curve and use them to form equations relating to the constants $ a $, $ b $, and $ c $.

The curve touches the $ x $-axis at $(-2, 0)$:

Since it touches the $ x $-axis at this point, the value of the function must be zero there:

$$ a(-2)^3 + b(-2)^2 + c(-2) + 5 = 0. $$

Simplifying this equation gives:

$$ -8a + 4b - 2c + 5 = 0.$$

Also, being a point of tangency implies the first derivative at this point is zero:

$$ 3ax^2 + 2bx + c = 0 $$ at $ x = -2 $.

Thus:

$$ 3a(-2)^2 + 2b(-2) + c = 0 $$

Simplifying:

$$ 12a - 4b + c = 0.$$

The gradient at the Y-intercept is 3:

The gradient of the curve is given by its first derivative, so at the point where the curve cuts the $ y $-axis (i.e., $ x = 0 $), the derivative is:

$$ 3ax^2 + 2bx + c = 3. $$

At $ x = 0 $:

$$ c = 3. $$

Using these three equations:

From the first equation:

$$ -8a + 4b - 2c + 5 = 0. $$

Substituting $ c = 3 $:

$$ -8a + 4b - 2(3) + 5 = 0 \implies -8a + 4b - 6 + 5 = 0 $$

$$ -8a + 4b - 1 = 0. \quad (1) $$

From the second equation:

$$ 12a - 4b + c = 0. $$

Again, substituting $ c = 3 $:

$$ 12a - 4b + 3 = 0. \quad (2) $$

Solve these equations simultaneously:

From equation (1):

$$ -8a + 4b = 1 $$

Then:

$$ b = 2a + \frac{1}{4} $$

Substitute in equation (2):

$$ 12a - 4(2a + \frac{1}{4}) + 3 = 0 $$

Expanding and simplifying:

$$ 12a - 8a - 1 + 3 = 0 $$

$$ 4a + 2 = 0 $$

$$ 4a = -2 $$

$$ a = -\frac{1}{2} $$

Now find $ b $:

$$ b = 2(-\frac{1}{2}) + \frac{1}{4} $$

$$ b = -1 + \frac{1}{4} $$

$$ b = -\frac{3}{4} $$

Now, sum $ a + b + c $:

$$ a + b + c = -\frac{1}{2} - \frac{3}{4} + 3 $$

Finding a common denominator:

$$ a + b + c = -\frac{2}{4} - \frac{3}{4} + \frac{12}{4} $$

$$ a + b + c = \frac{7}{4} $$

Thus, the value of $ a + b + c $ is $\boxed{\frac{7}{4}}$.

content: If $y=a \log x+b x^2+x$ has its extreme value at $x=-1$ and $x=2$, then the value of $a+b$ is
explanation:
To find the value of $a + b$, we need to determine the conditions for the given function $ y = a \log x + b x^2 + x $ to have extreme values at $ x = -1 $ and $ x = 2 $.

First, find the derivative of the function $ y $.

$ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}(a \log x + b x^2 + x) = \frac{a}{x} + 2bx + 1 $

Set the derivative equal to zero to find the critical points:

$ \frac{a}{x} + 2bx + 1 = 0 $

At the critical points $ x = -1 $ and $ x = 2 $:

Substitute $ x = -1 $:

$ \frac{a}{-1} + 2b(-1) + 1 = 0 \implies -a - 2b + 1 = 0 \implies a + 2b = 1 \quad \text{(Equation 1)} $

    Substitute $ x = 2 $:

    $ \frac{a}{2} + 2b(2) + 1 = 0 \implies \frac{a}{2} + 4b + 1 = 0 $

    Multiply this equation by 2 to eliminate the fraction:

    $ a + 8b + 2 = 0 \implies a + 8b = -2 \quad \text{(Equation 2)} $

    Now solve the system of equations formed by Equation 1 and Equation 2:

    Equation 1: $ a + 2b = 1 $

    Equation 2: $ a + 8b = -2 $

    Subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2 to eliminate $ a $:

    $ (a + 8b) - (a + 2b) = -2 - 1 $

    $ 6b = -3 \implies b = -\frac{1}{2} $

    Substitute $ b = -\frac{1}{2} $ into Equation 1:

    $ a + 2\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) = 1 $

    $ a - 1 = 1 \implies a = 2 $

    Finally, calculate $ a + b $:

    $ a + b = 2 - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{4}{2} - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{3}{2} $

    Thus, the value of $ a + b $ is $ \frac{3}{2} $.

    Option A: $\frac{3}{2}$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=x^3+b x^2+c x+d$ and $0< b^2< c$, then in $(-\infty, \infty)$
explanation:
To determine the nature of the function $$f(x) = x^3 + bx^2 + cx + d$$ in the entire domain of $(-\infty, \infty)$, we should analyze its derivative, as it provides insights into the monotonicity and critical points of the function.

The first derivative of the function is:

$$ f'(x) = 3x^2 + 2bx + c. $$

The critical points occur where the derivative is equal to zero, so we need to solve:

$$ 3x^2 + 2bx + c = 0. $$

The discriminant of this quadratic equation is given by:

$$ \Delta = (2b)^2 - 4(3)(c) = 4b^2 - 12c. $$

Given that $0 < b^2 < c$, we have:

$$ 4b^2 < 4c \implies 4b^2 - 12c < 0. $$

This implies $\Delta < 0$, meaning that the quadratic equation $3x^2 + 2bx + c = 0$ has no real roots. Consequently, $f'(x)$ does not change sign, and since the leading coefficient of $3x^2$ is positive, the parabola opens upwards. Hence, $f'(x) > 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

Since the derivative is always positive, the function $f(x)$ is strictly increasing over $(-\infty, \infty)$.

Therefore, the correct option is: Option A $f(x)$ is strictly increasing function.

content: The minimum value of the function $\mathrm{f}(x)=2 x^3-15 x^2+36 x-48$ on the set $\mathrm{A}=\left\{x \mid x^2+20 \leqslant 9 x\right\}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{A} & =\left\{x \mid x^2+20 \leq 9 x\right\} \\ & =\left\{x \mid x^2-9 x+20 \leq 0\right\} \\ & =\{x \mid(x-4)(x-5) \leq 0\} \\ \mathrm{A} & =\{4,5\} \\ \mathrm{f}(x) & =2 x^3-15 x^2+36 x-48 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) & =6 x^2-30 x+36 \\ & =6\left(x^2-5 x+6\right) \\ & =6(x-2)(x-3)<0 \forall x \in(4,5) \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \mathrm{f}(x)$ is strictly increasing in the interval $(4,5)$.

$\therefore \quad$ Minimum value of $\mathrm{f}(x)$ when $x \in(4,5)$ is $f(4)=-16$

content: The value of c for which Rolle's theorem for the function $\mathrm{f}(x)=x^3-3 x^2+2 x$ in the interval $[0,2]$ are
explanation:
Rolle's Theorem states that if a function $ f $ is continuous on the closed interval $[a, b]$, differentiable on the open interval $(a, b)$, and $ f(a) = f(b) $, then there exists at least one $ c $ in $(a, b)$ such that $ f'(c) = 0 $.

For the function $ f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 + 2x $ on the interval $[0, 2]$:

Verify Continuity and Differentiability:

$ f(x) $ is a polynomial, hence it is continuous and differentiable everywhere, including $[0, 2]$.

Check that $ f(a) = f(b) $:

Calculate $ f(0) $ and $ f(2) $:

$ f(0) = 0^3 - 3 \cdot 0^2 + 2 \cdot 0 = 0 $

$ f(2) = 2^3 - 3 \cdot 2^2 + 2 \cdot 2 = 8 - 12 + 4 = 0 $

Since $ f(0) = f(2) = 0 $, the conditions of Rolle's Theorem are satisfied.

Find $ c $ such that $ f'(c) = 0 $:

Compute the derivative $ f'(x) $:

$ f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(x^3 - 3x^2 + 2x) = 3x^2 - 6x + 2 $

Set the derivative equal to zero:

$ 3x^2 - 6x + 2 = 0 $

Solve the quadratic equation:

$ x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} = \frac{-(-6) \pm \sqrt{(-6)^2 - 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2}}{2 \cdot 3} $

$ x = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 24}}{6} = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{12}}{6} $

$ x = \frac{6 \pm 2\sqrt{3}}{6} = 1 \pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3} $

Therefore, the value of $ c $ that satisfies Rolle's Theorem is $ 1 \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} $.

Answer: Option C: $ 1 \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} $

content: A triangular park is enclosed on two sides by a fence and on the third side a straight river bank. The two sides having fence are of same length $x$. The maximum area (in sq. units) enclosed by the park is
explanation:
Let $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ be on isosceles triangle such that $$\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{AC}=x$$

$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{ABC}=\angle \mathrm{ACB}=\theta$

Draw seg $\mathrm{AD} \perp$ side BC at point D.

$\therefore \quad \triangle \mathrm{ABD}$ is a right angled triangle such that

$$\mathrm{AD}=x \sin \theta \text { and } \mathrm{BD}=x \cos \theta$$

Similarly, in $\triangle \mathrm{ACD}$,

$$\mathrm{DC}=x \cos \theta$$

$\therefore \quad$ In $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$,

Height $=\mathrm{AD}=x \sin \theta$

Base $=\mathrm{BC}=x \cos \theta+x \cos \theta=2 x \cos \theta$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{A}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC}) & =\frac{1}{2} \times x \sin \theta \times 2 x \cos \theta \\ & =\frac{x^2}{2}(2 \sin \theta \cos \theta) \\ & =\frac{x^2}{2} \sin 2 \theta \end{aligned}$$

Since, $-1 \leq \sin 2 \theta \leq 1$, for maximum value of $\sin 2 \theta$, Maximum area $=\frac{x^2}{2}$ sq. units.

content: A stone is dropped into a quiet lake and waves move in circles at speed of $8 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec}$. At the instant when the radius of the circular wave is 12 cm . how fast is the enclosed area increasing?
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given, the rate of increasing the radius }\\ &\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}=8 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec} \\ & \text { Area }=A=\pi r^2 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \pi \mathrm{r} \frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \pi(12)(8) \quad \ldots[\because \mathrm{r}=12 \mathrm{~cm}] \\ & =192 \pi \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the half life of substance is 5 years, then the total amount of the substance left after 15 years, when initial amount is 64 gms is
explanation:
Initial amount $=64 \mathrm{~gms}$

Half life period $=5$ years

$\therefore \quad$ Amount of substance left after 5 years $=\frac{64}{2}=32 \mathrm{~gms}$

Amount of substance left after 10 years $=\frac{32}{2}=16 \mathrm{~gms}$

Amount of substance left after 15 years $=\frac{16}{2}=8 \mathrm{~gms}$

content: A bullet is shot horizontally and its distance S cm at time t second is given by $\mathrm{S}=1200 \mathrm{t}-15 \mathrm{t}^2$, then the distance covered by the bullet when it comes to the rest, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & s=1200 t-15 t^2 \\ & \text { Velocity }(v)=\frac{d s}{d t}=1200-30 t \end{aligned}$$

When bullet stopped, $\frac{\mathrm{ds}}{\mathrm{dt}}=0$

$$\Rightarrow t=40$$

Hence, required distance

$$=1200(40)-15 \times(40)^2=24000 \mathrm{~cm}$$

content: The equation of the normal to the curve $x=\theta+\sin \theta, y=1+\cos \theta$ at $\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & x=\theta+\sin \theta, y=1+\cos \theta \\ & \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}=1+\cos \theta, \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}=-\sin \theta \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}}{\frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}}=\frac{-\sin \theta}{1+\cos \theta} \\ & \left.\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right|_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}}=\frac{-\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)}{1+\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)}=\frac{-1}{1+0}=-1 \\ & \text { At } \theta=\frac{\pi}{2} \text {, } \\ & x=\frac{\pi}{2}+\sin \frac{\pi}{2}=1+\frac{\pi}{2}, y=1+\cos \frac{\pi}{2}=1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \text { Equation of the normal is }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & (y-1)=\frac{-1}{\left(\left.\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right|_{0=\frac{\pi}{2}}\right)}\left(x-1-\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ \therefore & (y-1)=1\left(x-1-\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ \therefore & 2 x-2 y-\pi=0 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content: The co-ordinates of a point on the curve $y=x \log x$ at which the normal is parallel to the line $2 x-2 y=3$ are
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y=x \log x \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=1+\log x \end{aligned}$$

Slope of the normal $=-\frac{1}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)}=\frac{-1}{1+\log x}$

Slope of the given line is 1.

Since the normal is parallel to the given line.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{-1}{1+\log x}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \log x=-2 \\ & \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{e}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

From (i), $y=-2 \mathrm{e}^{-2}$

$\therefore \quad$ Co-ordinates of the point are $\left(\mathrm{e}^{-2},-2 \mathrm{e}^{-2}\right)$.

content: The value of C for which Mean value Theorem holds for the function $\mathrm{f}(x)=\log _e x$ on the interval $[1,3]$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \mathrm{f}(x) & =\log _{\mathrm{e}} x \\ \therefore\quad\mathrm{f}(1) & =\log _{\mathrm{e}} 1=0 \\ \mathrm{f}(3) & =\log _{\mathrm{e}} 3 \text { and } \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{x} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { By Lagrange's mean value theorem, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{c})=\frac{\mathrm{f}(3)-\mathrm{f}(1)}{3-1} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\log _{\mathrm{c}} 3-0}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\frac{2}{\log _{\mathrm{e}} 3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=2 \log _3 \mathrm{e} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content:
The maximum value of the function

$$f(x)=3 x^3-18 x^2+27 x-40$$

on the set $\mathrm{S}=\left\{x \in \mathbb{R} / x^2+30 \leq 11 x\right\}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{S}=\left\{x \in \mathbb{R} / x^2+30 \leq 11 x\right\} \\ & x^2+30 \leq 11 x \\ & \Rightarrow x^2-11 x+30 \leq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-5)(x-6) \leq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow x \in[5,6] \\ & \text { Now, } \mathrm{f}(x)=3 x^3-18 x^2+27 x-40 \\ & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=9 x^2-36 x+27 \\ & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=9\left(x^2-4 x+3\right) \\ & =9\left[\left(x^2-4 x+4\right)-1\right] \\ & \quad=9(x-2)^2-9 \\ & \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)>0 \forall x \in[5,6] \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x)$ is strictly increasing in the interval $[5,6]$

$\therefore \quad$ Maximum value of $\mathrm{f}(x)$ when $x \in[5,6]$ is

$$f(6)=122$$

content: The equation of normal to the curve $x=\theta+\sin \theta, y=1+\cos \theta$ at $\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & x=\theta+\sin \theta \text { and } y=1+\cos \theta \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}=1+\cos \theta \text { and } \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}=-\sin \theta \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}}{\frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}}=\frac{-\sin \theta}{1+\cos \theta} \\ & \text { At } \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}, \\ & x=\frac{\pi}{2}+\sin \frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{\pi}{2}+1 \text { and } \\ & y=1+\cos \frac{\pi}{2}=1 \\ \therefore \quad & \left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{\left(\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}\right)}=\frac{-\sin \frac{\pi}{2}}{1+\cos \frac{\pi}{2}}=-1 \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Slope of normal }=1 \end{array}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Equation of the normal at }\left(\frac{\pi}{2}+1,1\right) \text { is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & y-1=1\left(x-\frac{\pi}{2}-1\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 2 y-2=2 x-\pi-2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x-2 y-\pi=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The length of the longest interval, in which the function $3 \sin x-4 \sin ^3 x$ is increasing, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=3 \sin x-4 \sin ^3 x \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(x)=\sin 3 x \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=3 \cos 3 x \end{aligned}$$

For $f(x)$ to be increasing,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \cos 3 x \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos 3 x \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-\pi}{2} \leq 3 x \leq \frac{\pi}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-\pi}{6} \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ largest length in which $\mathrm{f}(x)$ is increasing

$$=\frac{\pi}{6}-\left(\frac{-\pi}{6}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}$$

content: If Rolle's theorem holds for the function $\mathrm{f}(x)=x^3+\mathrm{bx}{ }^2+\mathrm{ax}+5$ on $[1,3]$ with $\mathrm{c}=2+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$, then the values of $a$ and $b$ respectively are
explanation:
Since $\mathrm{f}(x)$ satisfies the Rolle's theorem, $\mathrm{f}(1)=\mathrm{f}(3)$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & 1+b+a+5=27+9 b+3 a+5 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 a+8 b=-26 \\ & \Rightarrow a+4 b=-13\quad\text{... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=x^3+\mathrm{bx}^2+\mathrm{a} x+5 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2+2 \mathrm{bx}+\mathrm{a} \end{aligned}$$

Now, $f^{\prime}(c)=0$

$\Rightarrow \mathbf{f}^{\prime}\left(2+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=0$

$\Rightarrow 3\left(2+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2+2 \mathrm{~b}\left(2+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)+\mathrm{a}=0$

$\Rightarrow 3\left(4+\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{1}{3}\right)+4 \mathrm{~b}+\frac{2 \mathrm{~b}}{\sqrt{3}}+\mathrm{a}=0$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}+4 \mathrm{~b}+\frac{2 \mathrm{~b}+12}{\sqrt{3}}+13=0$

$\Rightarrow-13+\frac{2 \mathrm{~b}+12}{\sqrt{3}}+13=0$ ... [From (i)]

$\Rightarrow \frac{2 \mathrm{~b}+12}{\sqrt{3}}=0$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{b}=-6$

Substituting $b=-6$ in (i), we get $a=11$

content: The normal to the curve, $y(x-2)(x-3)=x+6$ at the point, where the curve intersects the Y-axis, passes through the point
explanation:
Given equation of curve

$$\begin{aligned} & y(x-2)(x-3)=x+6 \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{x+6}{(x-2)(x-3)} \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{x+6}{x^2-5 x+6} \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =\frac{\left(x^2-5 x+6\right)(1)-(x+6)(2 x-5)}{\left(x^2-5 x+6\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{x^2-5 x+6-(x+6)(2 x-5)}{\left(x^2-5 x+6\right)^2} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} & =\frac{-x^2-12 x+36}{\left(x^2-5 x+6\right)^2} \end{aligned}$$

At Y -axis, $x=0$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}_\mathrm{at~x=0} & =\frac{-(0)^2-12(0)+36}{\left(0^2-5(0)+6\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{36}{36} \\ & =1 \end{aligned}$$

The equation of normal is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-1=-1(x-0) \\ & \text { i.e., } x+y=1 \end{aligned}$$

Option (B) i.e.,

$\therefore \quad\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$ satisfies above equation

$\therefore \quad$ Normal passes through $\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$.

content: The function $$\mathrm{f}(x)=x^3-6 x^2+9 x+2$$ has maximum value when $$x$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=x^3-6 x^2+9 x+2 \\ \therefore \quad & f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2-12 x+9 \end{aligned}$$

For maximum or minimum,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 x^2-12 x+9=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3(x-1)(x-3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=1,3 \end{aligned}$$

Now, $$\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(x)=6 x-12$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(1)=-6<0$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x)$$ is maximum at $$x=1$$.

content: If $$y=4 x-5$$ is a tangent to the curve $$y^2=\mathrm{p} x^3+\mathrm{q}$$ at $$(2,3)$$, then $$\mathrm{p}-\mathrm{q}$$ is
explanation:
$$y^2=p x^3+q$$ .... (i)

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{gathered} 2 y \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=3 \mathrm{p} x^2 \\ \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3 \mathrm{p}}{2}\left(\frac{x^2}{y}\right) \\ \therefore \quad\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{(2,3)}=\frac{3 \mathrm{p}}{2} \times \frac{4}{3}=2 \mathrm{p} \end{gathered}$$

Slope of the line $$y=4 x-5$$ is 4.

Since the line touches the curve, their slopes are equal.

$$\therefore \quad 2 \mathrm{p}=4 \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}=2$$

Since $$(2,3)$$ lies on $$y^2=\mathrm{p} x^3+\mathrm{q}$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 9=2 \times 8+q \Rightarrow q=-7 \\ & \therefore \quad p-q=2+7=9 \end{aligned}$$

content: The diagonal of a square is changing at the rate of $$0.5 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec}$$. Then the rate of change of area when the area is $$400 \mathrm{~cm}^2$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=0.5 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec} \\ & \text { Area }=\frac{x^2}{2} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{2 x}{2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=x \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{2} x \\ & {\left[\frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]_{\mathrm{A}=400}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{800}} \quad \cdots\left[\begin{array}{l} \because \mathrm{A}=400 \mathrm{~cm}^2 \\ x=\sqrt{800} \mathrm{~cm} \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$=10 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{sec}$$

content: Let $$x_0$$ be the point of local minima of $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})$$ where $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}=x \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+x \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}=7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+x \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$, then value of $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ at $$x=x_0$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \\ &=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x & -2 & 3 \\ -2 & x & -1 \\ 7 & -2 & x \end{array}\right| \\ &=x\left(x^2-2\right)+2(-2 x+7)+3(4-7 x) \\ \therefore & \quad\mathrm{f}(x)=x^3-27 x+26 \\ \end{aligned}$$

Now, $$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 3 x^2-27=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2=9 \\ & \Rightarrow x= \pm 3 \\ & \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(x)=6 x \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(x)=18>0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\mathrm{f}(x)$$ has local minimum at $$x=3$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \bar{a} & =3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \\ \bar{b} & =-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k} \\ \therefore \quad \bar{a} \cdot \bar{b} & =(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \\ & =3(-2)+(-2)(3)+3(-1) \\ & =-15 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let the curve be represented by $$x=2(\cos t+t \sin t), y=2(\sin t-t \cos t)$$. Then normal at any point '$$t$$' of the curve is at a distance of ______ units from the origin.
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & x=2(\cos t+t \sin t) \\ \therefore & \frac{d x}{d t}=2(-\sin t+\sin t+t \cos t)=2 t \cos t \\ & y=2(\sin t-t \cos t) \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d y}{d t}=2(\cos t-\cos t+t \sin t)=2 t \sin t \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{2 t \sin t}{2 t \cos t}=\tan t \end{array}$$

$$\text { Slope of normal }=-\frac{1}{\frac{d y}{d x}}=-\frac{1}{\tan t}=-\frac{\cos t}{\sin t}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Equation of the normal is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-2(\sin \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{t} \cos \mathrm{t})=-\frac{\cos \mathrm{t}}{\sin \mathrm{t}}[x-2(\cos \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{t} \sin \mathrm{t})] \\ & \Rightarrow y \sin \mathrm{t}-2 \sin ^2 \mathrm{t}+2 \mathrm{t} \sin \mathrm{t} \cos \mathrm{t} \\ & \quad=-x \cos \mathrm{t}+2 \cos ^2 \mathrm{t}+2 \mathrm{t} \sin \mathrm{t} \cos \mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow x \cos \mathrm{t}+y \sin \mathrm{t}=2\left(\sin ^2 \mathrm{t}+\cos ^2 \mathrm{t}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow x \cos \mathrm{t}+y \sin \mathrm{t}=2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad \text { Distance from origin }=\left|\frac{-2}{\sqrt{\cos ^2 t+\sin ^2 t}}\right|=2 \text { units }$$

content: Let $$\mathrm{B} \equiv(0,3)$$ and $$\mathrm{C} \equiv(4,0)$$. The point $$\mathrm{A}$$ is moving on the line $$y=2 x$$ at the rate of 2 units/second. The area of $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$ is increasing at the rate of
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } A=(h, 2 h) \\ & O A=\sqrt{h^2+4 h^2}=\sqrt{5} h \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d}(\mathrm{OA})}{\mathrm{dt}}=\sqrt{5} \frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \Rightarrow 2=\sqrt{5} \frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\alpha=\mathrm{A}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \mathrm{h} & 2 \mathrm{~h} & 1 \\ 0 & 3 & 1 \\ 4 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right|$$

$$\begin{aligned} = & \frac{1}{2}(3 \mathrm{~h}+8 \mathrm{~h}-12) \\ & =\frac{11 \mathrm{~h}-12}{2} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} \alpha}{\mathrm{dt}} & =\frac{11}{2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & =\frac{11}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \quad \text{.... [From (i)]}\\ & =\frac{11}{\sqrt{5}}\left(\text {units})^2 / \mathrm{sec}\right. \end{aligned}$$

content: The maximum value of the function $$f(x)=3 x^3-18 x^2+27 x-40$$ on the set $$\mathrm{S}=\left\{x \in \mathrm{R} / x^2+30 \leq 11 x\right\}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{S} & =\left\{x \in \mathrm{R} / x^2+30 \leq 11 x\right\} \\ & =\left\{x \in \mathrm{R} / x^2-11 x+30 \leq 0\right\} \\ & =\{x \in \mathrm{R} /(x-5)(x-6) \leq 0\} \\ & =\{x \in \mathrm{R} / x \in[5,6]\} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{f}(x)= & 3 x^3-18 x^2+27 x-40 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) & =9 x^2-36 x+27 \\ & =9(x-1)(x-3)>0 \quad \forall x \in[5,6] \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(x)$$ is increasing in $$[5,6]$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Maximum value } & =\mathrm{f}(6) \\ & =3(6)^3-18(6)^2+27(6)-40 \\ & =122 \end{aligned}$$

content: Slope of the tangent to the curve $$y=2 e^x \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right)$$, where $$0 \leq x \leq 2 \pi$$ is minimum at $$x=$$
explanation:
The slope of the tangent to the curve

$$\begin{aligned} y & =2 e^x \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right) \\ & =e^x \cos x \\ S & =\frac{d y}{d x}=e^x(-\sin x+\cos x) \end{aligned}$$

Now

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Now, } \frac{d S}{d x} & =e^x(-\sin x+\cos x-\cos x-\sin x) \\ & =-2 e^x \sin x \\ \frac{d S}{d x} & =0 \Rightarrow-2 e^x \sin x=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad x & =0, \pi, 2 \pi \quad(\because 0 \leq x \leq 2 \pi) \end{aligned}$$

Value of $$S$$ at $$x=0$$ is 1

Value of $$S$$ at $$x=\pi$$ is $$-e^\pi$$.

Value of $$S$$ at $$x=2 \pi$$ is $$e^{2 \pi}$$.

$$\therefore S$$ is minimum at $$x=\pi$$

content: If slope of the tangent to the curve $$x y+a x+b y=0$$ at the point $$(1,1)$$ on it is 2, then the value of $$3 a+b$$ is
explanation:
Given curve is $$x y+a x+b y=0$$

Slope of tangent at $$(1,1)$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+y+a+b \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\ & \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1,1}=-\left[\frac{a+y}{x+b}\right]_{(1,1)}=\frac{-(a+1)}{1+b} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{-(a+1)}{1+b}=2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{So,} \quad & 2+2 b=-a-1 \\ & a+2 b=-3 \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\because(1,1)$$ lies on curve $$x y+a x+b y=0$$.

$$\therefore \quad a+b=-1$$ .... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad b=-2, a=1 \\ \therefore & \quad 3 a+b=3 \times 1+(-2)=1 \end{aligned}$$

explanation:

We know that,

$$\Rightarrow \quad m_T =2$$
